stem: What establishment during the nineteenth century assisted in making athletic training as it is known today?
rationale: Athletic training as we know it came into existence during the late nineteenth century with the firm establishment of intercollegiate and interscholastic athletes in the United States.

stem: Which physician authored the first major text on athletic training titled The Trainer’s Bible?
rationale: Dr. S. E. Bilik, a physician who wrote the first major text in 1917 on athletic training and the care of athletic injuries called The Trainer’s Bible, was a major influence in developing the athletic trainer as a specialist in preventing and managing athletic injuries.

stem: What is the title of the monthly member magazine of the National Athletic Trainers’ Association?
rationale: NATA News is a monthly magazine published for members of the National Athletic Trainers’ Association.

stem: Which of the following organizations is “dedicated to transforming lives and revolutionizing the health and fitness industry”?
choices: American Physical Therapy Association, Sports Physical Therapy Section
rationale: The National Academy of Sports Medicine is dedicated to transforming lives and revolutionizing the health and fitness industry through its unwavering commitment to deliver innovative education, solutions, and tools that produce remarkable results.

stem: What organization has the position that all secondary schools should provide the services of a full-time, on-site, certified athletic trainers to student-athletes?
rationale: The medical delivery system for injured student-athletes needs a coordinator within the local school community who will facilitate the prevention, recognition, treatment, and reconditioning of sports-related injuries. Therefore, it is the position of the National Athletic Trainers’ Association that all secondary schools should provide the services of full-time, on-site, certified athletic trainer (ATC) to student-athletes.

stem: Which organization adopted a policy that “called for certified athletic trainers to be employed in all secondary-school athletic programs”?
choices: National Collegiate Athletic Association Committee on Competitive Safeguards and Medical Aspects of Sports
American Orthopedic Society for Sports Medicine
American Academy of Pediatrics, Sports Committee
rationale: The American Medical Association has “called for certified athletic trainers to be employed in all secondary-school athletic programs be used as part of a high school’s medical team.”

stem: What employment setting allows the athletic trainer to address appropriate workplace ergonomic techniques to reduce or minimize the possible risks for injury?
rationale: The athletic trainer working in an industrial or occupational setting must have a sound understanding of the principles and concepts of workplace ergonomics, including inspecting, measuring, and observing dimensions of the work space, as well as specific tasks that are performed at the work station.

stem: Of all the professional behaviors that an athletic trainer should possess, which would best describe the ability to adapt to the constant state of high emotional arousal and anxiety during the working day?
rationale: Athletic training requires abundant energy, vitality, and physical/emotional stability. Every day brings new challenges and problems that must be solved. The athletic trainer must be able to adapt to new situations with ease.

stem: Which of the following statements is an inaccurate ethical principle based on the NATA’s code of ethics?
choices: Members shall respect the rights, welfare, and dignity of all individuals.
Members shall accept responsibility for the exercise of sound judgment.
Members shall comply with the laws and regulations governing the practice of athletic training, National Athletic Trainers Association, NATA membership standards, and the NATA Code of Ethics.
Members shall maintain and promote high standards in their provision of services.
Members shall not engage in conduct that could be construed as a conflict of interest, reflects negatively on the athletic training profession, or jeopardizes a patient’s health and well-being.
rationale: In 2005, the NATA revised code of ethics. “Members shall accept responsibility for the exercise of sound judgment” was removed.

stem: Who determines the competencies that should be taught in entry-level education programs that are accredited by the CAATE?
choices: The Commission on Accreditation of Athletic Training Education (CAATE)
rationale: The Professional Education Committee determines the competencies that should be taught in entry-level education programs that are accredited by the CAATE.

stem: What would be considered an unessential duty of the team physician?
choices: diagnosing and treating injuries and other illnesses
advising an athlete about health matters
serving as an advisor to the athletic trainer
deciding when, on medical grounds, athletes should be disqualified from participation
serving as the medical director for an accredited athletic training program
rationale: In reference to Focus Box 1-6, serving as the medical director for an accredited athletic training program is not a listed duty for the team physician or a requirement for a CAATE-accredited athletic training program.

stem: Coaching is an area of specialization that is under the sports medicine “umbrella.” What medical role can this area serve?
choices: The coach is directly responsible for preventing injuries.
The coach is directly responsible for providing acute care such as CPR.
The coach is directly responsible for acting as a First Responder.
The coach is directly responsible for determining return to play.
The coach is directly responsible for monitoring inclement weather.
rationale: The coach is directly responsible for preventing injuries by seeing that athletes have undergone a preventive injury conditioning program. They must also ensure that sports equipment, especially protective equipment, is of the highest quality and is properly fitted.

stem: What state has no regulation in regard to athletic trainers?
rationale: As of 2023, California is the only state with no regulation for athletic trainers.

stem: What is the name of the organization whose purpose is to provide a certification program for entry-level athletic trainers and recertification standards for certified athletic trainers?
rationale: The Board of Certification’s purpose is to provide a certification program for entry-level athletic trainers and recertification standards for certified athletic trainers.

stem: When an athletic trainer treats a patient using evidence they received from the current best available evidence in professional literature it is called
rationale: Evidence-based practice is making decisions about the clinical care of individual patients based on the current best available evidence in the professional literature.

stem: Which of the following types of research would be considered the most rigorous?
rationale: Among the different types of research, randomized controlled trials (meta-analyses and systematic reviews) are the most rigorous.

stem: Which of the following represents what the “C” stands for in the acronym PICO?
rationale: C stands for Comparison. When developing a PICO, a comparison of the alternatives that might be used in the intervention should be completed.

stem: Which database currently holds the most comprehensive collection of systematic reviews for athletic trainers?
choices: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews
rationale: The Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews currently contains the largest database and is recommended that athletic trainers begin their search of systematic reviews here.

stem: Which of the following personal qualities are important for a good athletic trainer?
rationale: Empathy, intellectual curiosity, and a sense of humor are essential qualities for a good athletic trainer.

stem: Please identify which patient-related outcome measures are specifically used for pain.
choices: Disablement in the Physically Active Scale

stem: How does a clearly written mission statement influence the outcome of the strategic planning process when establishing a system for athletic training health care?
choices: It helps involve all personnel within the healthcare program.
It establishes why a healthcare program is needed.
It focuses the direction of the healthcare program.
It creates the goals of the healthcare program.
It takes a critical look at the strength and weaknesses of the healthcare program.
rationale: A clearly written mission statement will help focus the direction of the program and should be an outcome of the strategic planning process.

stem: What is the athletic training program’s primary purpose when performing a SWOT analysis?
choices: to discover weaknesses of staff skills and treatment protocols
to discover the potential of creating a brand-new athletic training program
to discover the program’s overall value of return based on patient treatments
to discover the program’s overall strengths, weaknesses, opportunities for growth, and threats to productivity
rationale: A SWOT analysis looks at Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats underlying planning. It is a useful and effective technique in strategic planning for existing athletic health care program.

stem: What is the primary purpose of creating a program policy and procedure manual for an athletic training program?
choices: to establish the daily order of operations of all staff members
to identify basic rules and principles used to control and expedite decision making
to define the scope of practice within the athletic training program and profession
to decide the extent to which patients will be served
to assign proper personnel coverage for the clinic population or specific sports
rationale: Policies are clear and accurate written statements that identify basic rules and principles used to control and expedite decision making. Procedures describe the process by which something is done.

stem: Within the scope of an athletic training program, what should be considered when offering medical services to members in the community?
choices: legality and the institution’s insurance program
age, gender, and ethnicity of the patient
proof of insurance for the patient
conflict of interest to local rehabilitation facilities
size and limitation of space within own facility
rationale: When making the decision to allow outside group(s) or people in the community be served by the athletic training staff, legality and the institution’s insurance program must be taken into consideration when making this decision.

stem: When deciding how to provide medical coverage for multiple athletic teams, what should the athletic trainer put into consideration when making the decision as to which team should receive on-site coverage?
choices: base it on distance to the athletic training room facility
provide for athletic teams that have potential of play-off contention
base it on potential risk and number of athletes involved
base it on number of injuries sustained during the last year
rationale: The athletic trainer in this difficult situation must make some decisions about where the greatest need for coverage is, based on the potential risk of a particular sport and the number of athletes involved.

stem: Of the following examples, which of the following would contribute to practicing unhealthy habits in an athletic setting?
choices: avoid drinking from a common water dispenser
be sure to change socks after any physical activity
avoid contact with teammates who have a contagious disease or infection
rationale: To promote good health among athletes, the athletic trainer should encourage sound health habits, such as avoid using a common towel.

stem: When developing a risk management plan, what would be considered a non-relevant issue to address?
rationale: Addressing OSHA rules and regulations would be more appropriately discussed in the athletic training policy and procedure manual.

stem: How often should electrical equipment be reevaluated, or calibrated?
choices: every time an accredited program goes up for re-accreditation
rationale: Electrical equipment should be reevaluated on a yearly basis and should conform to National Electrical Code guidelines. A clinic that is not in compliance with this code has no legal protection in a lawsuit.

stem: If an athletic trainer were to open a privately owned clinic, what would be an essential factor to consider when choosing a good location?
choices: Will doctors refer patients to this clinic?
Is there a public billboard near for advertising?
Will a local hospital help with your patient overflow?
Is there a physical therapy business that would refer clients?
Would an area doctor be willing to contract to hire you as a physician extender?
rationale: It is essential to build a patient base. Location is a key, but knowing an area physician would refer patients to you would help.

stem: Athletic trainers are becoming more diversified with job placement. What other athletic training duty involves providing intensive outpatient therapy for individuals injured on the job with the goal of regaining functionality and returning to work in a full duty capacity?
rationale: Athletic trainers may supervise or participate in work hardening or conditioning programs that address these needs. This can also include functional capacity evaluations to determine skill deficits as measured against physical job requirements.

stem: What is a disadvantage of using electronic medical records?
choices: Portability of documents allows access to multiple healthcare providers.
Cost factors are absorbed by the physician.
Errors are increased due to open access within an office.
They contradict the effectiveness of electronic health records.
rationale: The downside is that the improved portability and accessibility creates security issues that potentially threaten patient privacy. Electronic medical records improve overall efficiency, reduce errors, and reduce costs.

stem: What is the full meaning of the HIPAA abbreviation?
choices: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act
Health Insurance Process and Application Act
Health Is Private and Accessible Act
Health Insurance Portability and Application Act
rationale: The abbreviation for HIPAA is Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act.

stem: What is the Tanner Stage of maturity when a male has present: pubic hair becomes darker and coarser, begins to curl, and spreads over the pubic region?
rationale: The Tanner approach evaluates pubic hair and genitalia development in boys and pubic hair and breast development in girls. Stage 1 indicates that puberty is not evident, and stage 5 indicates full development.

stem: Who is the best suggested medical professional that should perform a clinical examination of heart and lung sounds on a patient during a preparticipation examination?
rationale: A physician should be the individual responsible for checking an athlete’s heart and lung sounds.

stem: What muscle is being assessed during the orthopedic screening if the patient is instructed to shrug the shoulders against the examiner’s resistance?
rationale: Performing a shoulder shrug against resistance is the manual muscle test for the trapezius.

stem: Doing a toe stand and heel walk will assist in determining ROM to what structure
rationale: Figure 2-8 identifies that a toe stand and heel walk will determine gastrocnemius ROM.

stem: Please check all of the following conditions that would recommend disqualification from a contact or collision sport.
choices: enlarged spleen K) absence of a testicle

stem: How many elements does the American Heart Association suggest are a part of the cardiovascular screen?
rationale: The 14-element cardiovascular screening checklist for congenital and genetic heart disease in young athletes is recommended by the American Heart Association.

stem: What system was developed to track football injuries through a Web-based system that tracks medical information and analyzes injury trends?
choices: Annual Survey of Football Injury Research
National Center for Catastrophic Sports Injury Research
rationale: The NCAA Injury Surveillance System (ISS) was established in 1982 primarily for the purpose of studying the incidence of football injuries. In Fall 2004, the ISS fully converted to a Web-based data-collection system that tracks medical information and analyzes injury trends.

stem: What are useful steps to promote good health among athletes?
choices: sleep a maximum of six hours a night
dry thoroughly and cool off before departing from the gymnasium
avoid contact with teammates who have a contagious disease or infection
understand the role coaches play in maintaining a healthy lifestyle and the prevention of injuries
rationale: There are many recommended actions that promote good health among athletes. The athletic trainer should encourage sound health habits and can disseminate this information to coaches and athletes.

stem: Which of the following would assist in proper cleanliness and sanitation in the athletic training clinic?
choices: No food should be allowed in the clinic
No cleated shoes should be permitted.
Game equipment should be allowed in the facility, however, must remain on the floor.
Athletes should make it a habit to shower after treatments
Roughhousing and profanity should not be allowed.
rationale: The athletic training clinic should be used only for the prevention and care of sports injuries. Unless definite rules are established and practiced, cleanliness and sanitation become an impossible chore.

stem: A patient states that they have a headache following a header in soccer. Where would this go in the SOAP note?
choices: it should go in none of these sections.
rationale: The subjective portion of the SOAP note refers to what the patient tells the athletic trainer about the injury relative to the history or what he or she felt.

stem: A patient is given a goal of running one mile in 2 weeks. Where would this go in the SOAP note?
choices: it should go in none of these sections.
rationale: The plan for treatment indicates how the injury will be managed and includes short- and long- term goals for rehabilitation.

stem: During an evaluation of a patient's knee you realize they are lacking 15 degrees of knee flexion. Where would this go in the SOAP note?
choices: it should go in none of these sections.
rationale: The objective portion documents information that the athletic trainer gathers during the evaluation, such as range of motion, strength levels, patterns of pain, and so forth.

stem: The Athletic Trainer believes that the athlete has a torn ACL based on the results of the Lachman’s test. Where would this go in the SOAP note?
choices: it should go in none of these sections.

stem: An athletic trainer decides to suture an athlete’s head wound which is against the state practice act. What type of legal concern is this?
rationale: When an individual commits an act that is not legally his to perform, it is an act of commission.

stem: Which of the following legal terms is synonymous with malfeasance?
rationale: Malfeasance can also be referred to as act of commission.

stem: An athletic trainer is performing ultrasound on a patient and accidentally burns the patient by failing to moving the ultrasound head. What type of legal concern is this?
rationale: When an individual improperly does something he or she has the legal right to perform, it is labeled as misfeasance.

stem: Please check all of the following things that an athletic trainer can do in order to reduce the risk of litigation.
choices: create an emergency action plan that will be reviewed every 3–5 years
keep factually accurate and timely records that at minimum document the patient’s injury
follow the express orders of a physician at all times only when the injury is severe
permit an injured player to participate once they look better
use common sense when making a decision about a patient’s health and safety
document efforts to create a safe training environment
work to establish good working relationships with the athletes, parents, patients, clients, and coworkers
obtain written consent from the team doctor to treat minors
be aware of the limitations of their personal liability insurance
only use modalities that state law permits

stem: What is the full meaning for the NOCSAE abbreviation?
choices: National Operating Commission on Safety of Athletic Equipment
National Operating Committee on Standards for Athletic Equipment
National Organization on Safety of Athletic Equipment
Newly Organized Committee on Standards for Athletic Equipment
Nations Operating Commission on Safety of Athletic Equipment
rationale: NOCSAE is the abbreviation for National Operating Committee on Standards for Athletic Equipment.

stem: What was developed to eliminate some of the worst practices of insurance companies and to put the consumer back in charge of their healthcare?
rationale: The Affordable Care Act included comprehensive health insurance reforms designed to eliminate some of the worst practices of insurance companies and to essentially put consumers back in charge of their health care.

stem: What type of insurance should an athletic trainer possess in order to cover the act of negligence in a civil case?
rationale: Professional liability insurance typically covers negligence in a civil case.

stem: Which of the following is an incorrect statement about catastrophic insurance?
choices: The medical plan benefits the NCAA offers to athletes who require a lifetime of extensive medical care because of a permanent disability.
Errors and omissions liability insurance evolved to offset the shotgun approach of lawsuits and to cover what is not covered by a general liability policy.
The National Federation of State High School Associations (NFSHSA) offers a plan that provides for costs in excess of $10,000 not covered by other insurance benefits.
The existence of these plans eliminates the need for the professional to carry personal liability insurance.
Costs for catastrophic insurance are based on the number of sports and the number of hazardous sports offered by the institution.
rationale: Even when working in a program that has good liability coverage, each person within that program who works directly with students must have his or her own personal liability insurance.

stem: Which third-party payer usually involves a major hospital or hospital chain and its physicians?
rationale: Physician Hospital Organizations (PHO) also contract directly with employers to provide services and/or contracts with a managed care organization.

stem: Which third-party payer is a combination of the HMO and PPO plans, yet allows members to go outside the HMO to obtain services?
rationale: The Point of Service plans allows flexibility only with certain conditions and under special circumstances.

stem: Which third-party payer would provide financial medical assistance to an athlete who comes from a low-income family?
rationale: Medicaid is funded by both the federal government and individual states, with the state responsible for handling the administration of the program; therefore, benefits vary by state.

stem: When were athletic trainers able to bill third-party payers for services rendered?
choices: Athletic trainers have always been able to bill.
rationale: Athletic trainers have always been able to bill third-party payers for services rendered. The 1990s and early 2000s saw a significant increase in reimbursement.

stem: What needs to be stressed to parents if the host institution offers a secondary insurance policy for athletes?
choices: Save all receipts so they can be filed with the individual’s tax records.
Submit all bills to the primary insurance company before submitting the remainder to the host institution.
All insurance filing will be taken care of by the host institution.
Pay all bills up front; the host institution will reimburse the family.
Keep all the bills until the end, then collectively file them together.
rationale: Schools with secondary policies should stress that the parents must submit all bills to their insurance company before they submit the remainder to the school.

stem: What action by the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) in 2005 created a job loss for athletic trainers working in clinics and hospitals or as physician extenders?
choices: Individual state licensure acts were revoked; Board of Certification was the only honoring professional credentialing body.
Insurance billing codes for athletic training rehabilitation services were deleted.
A mandate was set forth that only outcomes research practices/treatments could be eligible for reimbursement.
Athletic trainers were no longer recognized as a qualified professional to provide therapy incident to a physician’s service.
Reimbursement could only be offered if the patient had primary insurance coverage.
rationale: The CMS ruling stated that they would “no longer pay for therapy ‘incident to’ a physician’s services unless the provider is a physical therapist, occupational therapist, or speech/language pathologist.”

stem: What type of code identifies specific medical procedures used in treating a patient?
rationale: Current Procedure Terminology Code (CPT) was first developed by the American Medical Association in 1966. Annual publications designate changes corresponding to significant updates in medical technology and practice.

stem: Please check all of the items that an Athletic Trainer can bill for based on procedure codes.
What does the Sports Medicine Licensure Clarity Act protect?
choices: The act protects the athlete’s ability to choose a healthcare provider.
The act protects the right to practice athletic training.
Ensuring that athletic trainers and other sports medicine professionals are properly covered by malpractice insurance while traveling.
Insuring that athletic trainers can bill for service.
HIPPA and FERPA must be protected for all athletes.
rationale: The Sports Medicine Licensure Clarity Act was passed in 2017 and the NATAPAC was directly responsible for that passage.

stem: Any healthcare provider who uses standard electronic transactions, such as electronic claims, eligibility verifications, claims status inquiries, and claim attachments is required to have one of these.
rationale: As of 2007, any healthcare provider who uses standard electronic transactions, such as electronic claims, eligibility verifications, claims status inquiries, and claim attachments is required to have an NPI number.

stem: When submitting documentation for billing, what needs to be included? Please check all that apply

You are hosting a flag football tournament and your coach comes to you asking what type of insurance coverage you need to cover the students at this event. What would be the most appropriate insurance?
rationale: It covers accidents on school grounds while the student is in attendance or accidents that occur in the workplace; however, the coverage is limited and often does not cover surgery or lengthy rehabilitations.

stem: Which of the following legal terms is synonymous with nonfeasance?
rationale: Nonfeasance can also be referred to as act of omission.

stem: In order to prove negligence, the complaint must establish which of the following things?
choices: A duty of care existed between the person injured and the person responsible for that injury.
The defendant breached this duty by conduct that fell short of the standard of care.
The defendant caused the injury to occur.
Personal, property or punitive damages resulted.
The injured person was not following directions.
The injured person did not report the incident to the athletic trainer.
The defendant followed the emergency action plan.
rationale: Focus Box 3-1 identifies what an athletic trainer can do to avoid claims of negligence.

stem: You have an athlete who fractured their arm while playing basketball. Their insurance has paid most of the bill; however, they still have an out of pocket expense of $1000.00. What type of policy might your institution have to pay this bill?
Which of the following is a common role that the athletic trainer has to fulfill when employed in the secondary school setting?
rationale: In the majority of secondary school settings, a strength and conditioning coach is not available; the responsibility for ensuring that the athlete gets fit lies with the athletic trainer and the team coaches.

stem: What principle of conditioning addresses an individual’s specific adaptation to imposed demands?
rationale: Logan and Wallis identified the SAID principle, which directly relates to the principle of overload. SAID – specific adaptation to imposed demands.

stem: Which of the following principles of conditioning allows the program designer to address group needs, rather than individual needs?
rationale: The other principles of conditioning are based on the individual’s needs or physical abilities. Consistency is having a regularly scheduled basis for strength and conditioning to be effective.

stem: What type of activity involves continuous movement using hopping, skipping, and bounding activities with several different footwork drills?
rationale: A dynamic warm-up involves continuous movement using hopping, skipping, and bounding activities with several different footwork drills and patterns.

stem: What four components coordinate together to transport oxygen throughout the body?
rationale: Basically, transport of oxygen throughout the body involves the coordinated function of four components: the heart, the lungs, the blood vessels, and the blood.

stem: The volume of blood being pumped out with each beat is called
rationale: Stroke volume is the volume of blood being pumped out with each beat.

stem: How is the heart able to adapt to increased demands during exercise?
choices: It decreases the volume of blood being pumped out with each beat.
The heart’s rate will plateau at a given level within 15–20 minutes.
With high-intensity demands, heart rate and oxygen consumption are inversely related.
Cardiac output increases as aerobic capacity increases.
The stroke volume will increase as the heart rate increases at a given exercise load.
rationale: During exercise, cardiac output increases to approximately four times that experienced during rest in the normal individual and may increase as much as six times in the elite endurance athlete.

stem: What is known as the “ultimate usable form of energy for muscular activity”?
rationale: Energy is produced from the breakdown of nutrient foodstuffs. This energy is used to produce adenosine triphosphate (ATP).

stem: Check only the steps that contribute to the energy system of anaerobic metabolism.
choices: The body turns to stored glycogen as an energy source.
A by-product, glucose, seeps out of the muscle cells into the blood to be used elsewhere.
ATP is rapidly metabolized to meet energy needs.
Lactate is broken down to supply glucose, which is then metabolized within the muscle cells to generate ATP for muscle contractions without the need for oxygen.
During sudden outbursts of activity in intensive, short-time exercise, ATP is stored to meet energy needs.
rationale: Lactic acid is the by-product that seeps out of the muscle cells into the blood. Glycogen is broken down to supply glucose. And, during sudden bursts of activity that intense, ATP is metabolized to meet energy needs.

stem: What is broken down to supply glucose?
rationale: Glycogen is broken down to supply glucose, which is then metabolized within the muscle cells to generate ATP for muscle contractions without the need for oxygen.

stem: Which of the following activities is an example of interval training?
choices: sprinting the straight-aways and jogging the curves of a track
running from home plate to second base 15 times
cariocas on a football field for 15 minutes
rationale: “Fartlek” training is a type of interval training that puts surges into a running workout, varying the length of the surges according to individual purposes.

stem: What term is best identified with enlargement of a muscle caused by an increase in the size of its cells?
rationale: Hypertrophy is the enlargement of a muscle caused by an increase in the size of its cells in response to training.

stem: Will a muscle hypertrophy if myofilaments also increase in both size and number?
choices: Yes, this supports the theory that the number of muscle fibers increases because fibers split in response to training.
No, this contradicts the theory that myofilament increase only occurs in males.
Yes, this supports the theory that more blood is required to supply the muscle, thus, also increasing the number of capillaries.
No, this contradicts the theory that the number of fibers is genetically determined and does not increase with training.
Yes, this supports the theory that individual muscle fibers themselves increase in cross-sectional diameter.
rationale: A third theory to explain muscle hypertrophy is that muscle fibers are composed primarily of small protein filaments, called myofilaments, which are contractile elements in muscle.

stem: Select the correct explanation of the length-tension relation in muscle based on the figure.
choices: Point A is the interaction where the crossbridges between the actin and myosin myofilaments within the sarcomere are at their maximum.
Point B is the interaction where the crossbridges between the actin and myosin myofilaments within the sarcomere are at their maximum.
Point C is the interaction where the crossbridges between the actin and myosin myofilaments within the sarcomere are at their maximum.
Points A and C have the greatest amount of tension.
Points B and C have the greatest amount of tension.
rationale: The length of a muscle determines the tension that can be generated. By varying the length of a muscle, different tensions may be produced.

stem: What determines the tension that can be generated when referring to muscular strength?
rationale: The length of the muscle determines the tension that can be generated. In addition to the position of attachment of the muscle, tendon on the arm can affect the ability of that muscle to generate force.

stem: How does an isometric muscular contraction occur?
choices: The muscle shortens in length as the contraction is developed to overcome or move some resistance.
The muscle contracts to increase tension but there is no change in the length of the muscle.
The muscle contracts to increase tension with a change in the length of the muscle.
The muscle contracts while being elongated but does not change in length.
The muscle shortens in length, and then elongates.
rationale: An isometric contraction does not have a change in muscle length even though the muscle contracts.

stem: Which of the following techniques of resistance training help an individual gain strength, neuromuscular control, power, and muscle endurance?
rationale: The concept of core stability training is essential. A core stabilization training program is designed to address the muscles in the lumbar spine, the abdomen, and around the hip and pelvis. These muscles are collectively referred to as the core.

stem: What type of exercise is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: An isometric exercise involves a muscle contraction in which the length of the muscle remains constant while tension develops toward a maximal force against an immovable resistance.

stem: What type of exercise is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: A core stabilization training program is designed to help an individual gain strength, neuromuscular control, power, and muscle endurance of the muscles in the lumbar spine, abdomen, and around the hip and pelvis.

stem: Check only the exercises that can be categorized as a functional exercise.
choices: front lunge balance to one-arm press
tandem stance on Dyna disc with trunk rotation
stability ball diagonal rotations with weighted ball
rationale: Functional strengthening exercises use simultaneous movement (concentric, eccentric, and isometric contractions) in three planes on both stable and unstable surfaces.

stem: Shortening and lengthening a muscle through a complete range of motion for which of the following types of exercise?
rationale: In an isotonic exercise, the muscle shortens and lengthens through a complete range of motion.

stem: What type of exercise is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: Plyometric exercise is a technique that includes specific exercises that encompass a rapid stretch of a muscle eccentrically, followed immediately by a rapid concentric contraction of that muscle for the purpose of facilitating and developing a forceful explosive movement over a short period of time.

stem: If you were to combine all of these activities into a series of stations that would be repeated three times, what would it be called?
rationale: Circuit training consists of exercise stations that consist of various combinations of weight training, flexibility, calisthenics, and aerobic exercises.

stem: Which of the following factors could limit flexibility?
What type of stretching is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: Static stretching is a technique widely used and effective technique of stretching involving passively stretching a given antagonist muscle by placing it in a maximal position of stretch and holding it there for an extended time.

stem: What type of stretching is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: Dynamic stretching is controlled stretches recommended prior to beginning an activity.

stem: What type of stretching is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: Myofascial stretching stretches tight fascia and can be done manually or using a firm foam roller.

stem: What instrument/machine is most commonly used to measure joint angles and range of motion at a joint?
rationale: A goniometer is the simplest and most widely used instrument to measure joint angles and range of motion.

stem: A patient has a rating of perceived exertion of 19. What would be their verbal rating?
rationale: The rating of perceived exertion chart (Table 4-2) identified 19 as a very, very hard rating.

stem: What plateaus at 40% of the maximum heart rate?
rationale: Figure 4-4 identifies that stroke volume plateaus at 40% of the maximal heart rate

stem: Which of the following is an example of high-intensity interval training?
choices: a basketball player shooting 5 balls in 1 minute
a cross-country runner completing 3 miles in 30 minutes
a volleyball player completing 10 serves in 2 minutes
rationale: Figure 4-7 identifies an example of %HRR of a soccer player running sprints.

stem: What causes a reflex relaxation of the muscle in response to stretch?
choices: type Ia nerve from muscle spindle

stem: Please select only the major class of nutrients by clicking the appropriate box.
Which of the following nutritional energy sources is needed for growth, maintenance, and repair of all body tissues?
rationale: Proteins make up the major structural component of the body and are needed for growth, maintenance, and repair of all body tissues.

stem: What vitamin when taken in excess can cause kidney stones and diarrhea?
rationale: An excess of vitamin C consumption can cause kidney stones and diarrhea.

stem: What vitamin when taken in excess can cause headaches, nausea, loss of hair, dry skin, and diarrhea?
rationale: An excess of vitamin A consumption can cause headaches, nausea, loss of hair, dry skin, and diarrhea.

stem: Which of the following vitamin(s) should not be taken on a daily basis? 1. Vitamin A 2. Vitamin D 3. Thiamin 4. Vitamin C 5. Riboflavin
rationale: Vitamin A and Vitamin D are fat-soluble; therefore, do not need to be taken on a daily basis.

stem: Which of the following mineral deficiencies will produce skin problems and growth problems?
rationale: A deficiency in zinc can cause all of the following: skin problems, delayed development, and growth problems.

stem: Which of the following mineral supplements will assist in preventing osteoporosis?
rationale: Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the body and is essential for bones and teeth. Because osteoporosis is a decrease in bone density, increasing calcium intake can help.

stem: A patient is suffering from the following symptoms: depression, fever, vomiting, paranoid psychosis, and significant weight loss. What is the suspected supplement being ingested?
rationale: Ephedrine is a stimulant that has been used as an ingredient in diet pills. The FDA banned the use of ephedrine as a dietary supplement. Ephedrine is also known to produce tachycardia, depression, fever, vomiting, and significant weight loss.

stem: Which of the following nutritional supplements has an integral role in energy metabolism and is synthesized by the kidneys, liver, and pancreas?
rationale: Creatine is a naturally occurring organic compound synthesized by the kidneys, liver, and pancreas.

stem: What is an advantage to utilizing ultrasound to measure the tissue thickness of muscle and intra-abdominal depth?
choices: Ultrasound is cheaper than skin calipers.
Ultrasound does a better job of measuring superficial fat.
Ultrasound has the best results on individuals with a low BMI.
Ultrasound can get at locations where calipers cannot be applied.
rationale: An advantage of ultrasound compared with skinfold measurement is the possibility to measure obese subjects and to reach anatomical locations where calipers cannot be applied.

stem: In order to maximize glycogen resynthesis when is it recommended that a person ingests dietary carbohydrates and protein following activity?
rationale: It is recommended that to maximize glycogen resynthesis, both dietary carbohydrates and protein should be ingested immediately following exercise.

stem: What type of vegetarian dietary pattern consumes dairy products and eggs in the diet along with plant foods; meat, fish, and poultry are excluded?
rationale: The practice of lactovegetarianism could compromise the ability to obtain sufficient levels of iron.

stem: When designing the pregame, or prevent meal, what should be excluded as a consideration?
choices: eating preferences of the individual athlete
ease of digestibility of the food
consumption three to four hours prior to event/game
achieve largest possible storage of carbohydrates
rationale: Liquid consumed should be easily absorbed and low in fat content and should not act as a laxative. Water intake should be increased, particularly if the temperature is high.

stem: Please check all of the following food items that would be ideal for an afternoon pregame meal.
Which of the following items that you consume has the highest amount of calories per gram?
rationale: Fat has the highest amount of calories, which is 9 per gram.

stem: For an athlete competing in endurance events, what nutritional practice can maximize the amount of glycogen that can be stored?
rationale: Glycogen supercompensation is a high-carbohydrate diet that restores glycogen levels in muscle and the liver on the basis that stored glycogen in the muscles directly affects the endurance of that muscle.

stem: Please check all of the areas where you should check body composition utilizing skin calipers.
Which assessment method provides one of the most accurate measurements of body composition?
rationale: Hydrostatic measurement, if done properly, is very accurate.

stem: Which of the following is a misrepresentation of caloric value?
rationale: Sugar is a carbohydrate, thus equaling 4 calories / gram.

stem: What is the correct technique for measuring basal metabolic rate (BMR)?
choices: Fasting for 12 hours, awakening in a quiet, warm environment, and measuring O2 uptake using indirect calorimetry.
Exhaling completely before being immersed in an underwater tank while holding the breath.
Measuring 3–5 sites on the body using a Skinfold caliper.
Securely holding a bioelectrical unit with both hands until the measurement is read.
Obtaining height and weight and plugging those values into a formula.
rationale: Basal metabolic rate is the rate at which calories are spent for sustaining the body’s vital functions such as respiration, heartbeat, circulation, and maintenance of body temperature during a 24-hour period.

stem: What are the recommended values for gaining one pound of muscle and for losing one pound of fat?
choices: 1,500 calories to gain one pound of muscle; 2,500 calories to lose one pound of fat
2,000 calories to gain one pound of muscle; 3,000 calories to lose one pound of fat
2,500 calories to gain one pound of muscle; 3,500 calories to lose one pound of fat
3,000 calories to gain one pound of muscle; 4,000 calories to lose one pound of fat
3,500 calories to gain one pound of muscle; 4,500 calories to lose one pound of fat
rationale: To gain one pound of muscle, an excess of approximately 2,500 calories is needed; to lose one pound of fat, approximately 3,500 calories in excess of intake must be expended in activities.

stem: Which eating disorder has a distorted body image and concern of weight gain, but is set apart by the lack of self-starvation practices?
rationale: Anorexia athletics is a condition specific to athletes that is characterized by several of the features common to anorexia nervosa, but without self-starvation practices.

stem: Please check all of the foods that have a glycemic index greater than 70.
choices: whole grain cereal K) ice cream

stem: Please check the nutritional deficiencies that could occur due to a gluten-free diet.

How can this statement about heat stress be corrected? While heat-related illnesses most often occur in hot, humid, sunny conditions, it is impossible for individuals training in cold environments to be susceptible to heat stress.
choices: Heat-related illnessesalways occur in hot, humid, sunny conditions.
Cold environments are susceptible to heat stress if the sweating mechanism is inhibited due to protective equipment.
Heat stress is not preventable no matter the environment; it is related to the level of physical activity.
Cold-related heat problems occur because of failure to acclimatize.
rationale: Heat-related illnesses in cold environments can also occur if an individual becomes dehydrated.

stem: What is not a way heat can be gained or lost through the body?
rationale: The fifth way that heat can be lost or gained through the body is convection heat exchange.

stem: Is heredity considered a factor when trying to determine an individual’s sweat rate?
choices: yes, along with race and gender
no, but height and weight, along with hydration status are
yes, along with cardiac output and color of clothing
no, but acclimatization and fitness level are
yes, along with intensity, duration of activity, and environmental conditions
rationale: Sweat rates can vary considerably from one athlete to another and are determined by a number of factors, Focus Box 6-1.

stem: How does a high humidity level alter the sweat evaporation process making an individual susceptible to a heat illness?
choices: The extra moisture in the air that is absorbed by the body overloads the body’s evaporation process.
Sweat is not able to evaporate because of the excessive moisture in the air, not allowing heat to be dissipated from the body.
The absorbed moisture in the air increases the amount of sweat lost, dehydrating the body.
The combination of moisture in the air and sweat causes excessive sodium levels, shutting down the evaporation process.
Environmental moisture, combined with the body’s sweat loss, blocks the sweat glands, disabling the ability for moisture to exit the body.
rationale: The air must be relatively free of water for evaporation to occur. Heat loss through evaporation is severely impaired when the relative humidity reaches 65% and virtually stops when the humidity reaches 75%.

stem: What is an INCORRECT statement from the “NATA recommendations for preventing heat illness”?
choices: Ensure that appropriate medical care is available.
Educate athletes to balance fluid intake with sweat and urine losses to maintain adequate hydration.
Encourage athletes to sleep six to eight hours per night in a cool environment.
Minimize the amount of equipment and clothing worn in hot humid conditions.
Extend warm-up time and minimize practice periods in hot humid conditions.
rationale: Warm-up time should be minimized in hot humid conditions, Focus Box 6-2.

stem: Why would an athletic trainer advise an athlete to consume a little more than a 2-liter soda bottle full of water?
choices: Soda is a stimulant that will help provide energy throughout the day.
Two-and-a-half liters of fluid is the equivalent of twenty glasses of water a day.
An active athlete would need to consume that much to replace the sweat-loss rate after exercising for an hour.
It is easier to relate consumption size to something common so the athlete will understand daily fluid intake.
That is how much fluid needs to be replaced for every pound lost due to sweat-rate loss.
rationale: The average adult doing minimal physical activity requires a minimum of 2.5 liters of water or about ten glasses of water a day.

stem: In preventing heat illnesses, what statements should be endorsed?
choices: When body weight drops by 1–2%, mild dehydration has already occurred.
A 14% carbohydrate solution appears to be optimal when trying to replace fluids.
An athlete should consume 17–20 ounces of water or a sport drink four to five hours before exercise.
Urine should appear clear in color, indicating low levels of ketosis.
Progressive heat exposure should occur over a five- to six-day period.
rationale: Even mild hydration can impair cardiovascular and thermoregulatory response, can reduce the capacity for exercise, and have a negative effect on performance.

stem: Which of the following statements about the universal wet bulb globe temperature is incorrect?
choices: GT = measurement of the sun's radiation
rationale: The correct formula for WBGT index if only a wet bulb and dry bulb temperature are taken: WBGT = [0.3 × DBT] + [0.7 ×WBT].

stem: What is normal rectal temperature?
rationale: Normal rectal temperature is 99.6°F (37.5°C) and temperature associated with heat stroke is ≥ 105°F. This mode of temperature measurement is necessary when needing to differentiate between heat exhaustion and heat stroke.

stem: What are the correct steps to be taken when measuring rectal temperature?
choices: Place the athlete's stomach down with both legs spread apart.
If using a flexible digital thermometer, tape can be used to secure it in place.
Spread the buttocks and gently insert the thermometer 8 to 10 inches into the rectum.
Leave the thermometer in place for 3 minutes.
Cover the tip of the glass thermometer or a flexible digital thermometer with lubricating or petroleum jelly.
Do not hold a glass thermometer while obtaining the temperature to avoid altering the patient’s temperature.
rationale: The athlete, while prone, should flex up one leg for easier access and the thermometer should only be inserted 3 to 4 inches. Additionally, a glass thermometer needs to be held onto while obtaining a temperature.

stem: What heat illnesses is a syndrome that is characterized by sudden catabolic destruction and degeneration of skeletal muscle?
rationale: Acute exertional rhabdomyolysis is also accompanied by leakage of myoglobin (muscle protein) and muscle enzymes into the vascular system. It can occur in healthy individuals during intense exercise in extremely hot and humid environmental conditions.

stem: Please check all of the following signs and symptoms of hyponatremia
choices: swelling in the hands and feet

stem: What common structural damage of the body connects malignant hyperthermia and acute exertional rhabdomyolysis?
rationale: Malignant hyperthermia is characterized by muscle breakdown where these by-products may damage the kidneys and cause acute renal failure. Acute exertional rhabdomyolysis is degeneration of skeletal muscle and leakage of myoglobin into the vascular system that result in renal failure.

stem: What does this apparatus measure?
choices: It is a lightning detector to detect lightning and the level of activity of the storm and its approximate distance.
It is a thermometer to measure rectal temperature.
It is a temperature gun to determine ambient temperature.
It is a digital psychrometer used to determine wet-bulb globe temperature heat index.
It is a sling psychrometer used to determine wet-bulb temperature.
rationale: This is a portable digital psychrometer used to determine the WBGT heat index.

stem: An individual is running outside and wind is at 30 mph. What temperature would be considered imminent for an onset of frostbite to occur within 30 minutes?
rationale: Utilize the provided figure.

stem: A track athlete will be traveling to Alaska for two weeks for training. What advice would you give this athlete regarding their training regimen?
choices: Always wear heavy clothing while out in the cold.
Sweating does not occur in cold climates; therefore, train longer and harder.
Wear several heavy layers once the temperature reaches below 50°F.
The body loses most of its heat through evaporation; therefore, wear clothing that collects sweat and heat.
Wear lightweight insulated clothing that prevents dampness and allows a chilling factor after activity.
rationale: Runners who wish to continue outdoor work in cold weather should use lightweight insulated clothing that permits the free passage of sweat and body heat that would otherwise accumulate on the skin or the clothing, and provides a chilling factor when activity ceases. If breathing cold air seems distressful, should use ski goggles and a ski face mask or should cover the mouth and nose with a free-hanging cloth.

stem: A soccer game is about to start when a bolt of lightning lights the sky. Thirty seconds passes before a sound is heard. Using the flash-to-bang theory approximately how many miles away is the storm?
rationale: Flash-to-bang is the number of seconds from lightning flash until the sound of thunder is divided by five to determine the distance from the lightning strike.

stem: What organ can rupture with a sickle cell trait reaction when at high altitudes?
rationale: Sickle cell trait reaction can cause an enlarged spleen, which, in some cases, has been known to rupture at high altitudes.

stem: In order to minimize jet lag for an athletic team having to travel from New York to Europe, what option would be the best advice?
choices: stay awake as long as possible in order to rest during the flight
eat the heaviest meal for dinner
rationale: Minimizing the effects of jet lag can occur by drinking plenty of fluids to avoid dehydration, which occurs because of dry, high-altitude, low-humidity cabin air.

stem: What statement regarding synthetic turf versus natural surfaces is correct?
choices: According to the literature, synthetic surfaces are cooler in ambient temperatures than natural surfaces.
According to the literature, natural surfaces are more likely to cause injury than synthetic surfaces.
According to the literature, turf toe injuries are prevalent.
According to the literature, synthetic surfaces produce less friction, thus decreasing injuries to natural surfaces.
According to the literature, natural surfaces have a lower concussion rate to synthetic surfaces.
rationale: From an injury perspective, turf toe and abrasions are more prevalent with the use of synthetic surface.

stem: Please check all of the following methods that would be useful in preventing a heat injury (Focus 6-6).
choices: Acclimatize the individual to the heat within the first 24 hours
If an individual suffers from pallor or is cyanotic in appearance, they should stop activity
Have the individual wear long stockings and long sleeves
Avoid placing athletes near brick walls
Measure temperature and humidity on the practice or playing field
Identify individuals that are susceptible to heat illness
Have all athletes take salt tablets
Weigh all athletes after the final training session of the day
If an athlete suffers from diarrhea and nausea, they should practice until, they begin vomiting

stem: Please check all of the following items that should be included in an Emergency Action Plan pertaining to lightning safety. (Focus Box 6-7)
choices: If shelter cannot be found have individuals stand under umbrellas
Allow 20 minutes to pass after the last sound of thunder before resuming play
If the thunder and lightning can be seen and the flash to bang is 30 or less, you should seek shelter
If you feel your hair start to stand on end and skin tingle, you should lie flat on the ground
Seek shelter in a building if lightning has been spotted
If no shelter is available, an equipment shed would be a good alternative
If no shelter is available, seeking shelter under a tree would be a good alternative
Individuals should avoid standing water at all times
A lightning detector could be used to detect a lightning storm within 5 miles and would assist in providing information in regards to a storm

stem: Which of the following statements are correct for recommendations for fluid replacement?
choices: A 6% carbohydrate solution appears to be optimal, greater than 8% slows gastric emptying.
A cool, flavored beverage with a small amount of caffeine will assist in boosting energy levels.
Adding at least one gram of sodium to one liter of fluid is acceptable to stimulate thirst and increase fluid intake.
To ensure proper hydration, one should consume 17–20 oz of water or a sport drink 2–3 hours before exercise and then 7–10 oz 10–20 minutes before exercise.
During exercise, consumption of fluids should be at the rate of 10–20 ounces for every hour.
rationale: Caffeine is a diuretic. Exercising for at least 50 minutes necessitates the need for carbohydrate and electrolyte fluid replacement, lastly, consumption of fluids is 7–10 oz every 10–20 minutes during exercise. Adding 0.3–0.7 grams of sodium per liter of fluid is acceptable.

stem: Death from heatstroke increases at what ratio as body weight increases?
rationale: Death from heatstroke increases at a ratio of approximately four to one as body weight increases.

stem: What is the recommended water replacement per hour for heat category 3 with a WBGT °F of 85-87.9, No limit, Easy work level?
rationale: Based on the Universal WBGT Index Fluid Replacement Recommendations for heat category 3 with a WBGT °F of 85–87.9, no limit for work/rest period, and easy work level, water replacement should be ¾ quart per hour.

stem: What physiological event occurs with exercise-associated muscle cramps?
choices: Muscle spindle activity increases while Golgi tendon organ activity decreases.
Muscle spindle activity decreases while Golgi tendon organ activity increases.
A reflex relaxation or cramp of a muscle occurs.
More commonly associated with stabilizing, low-demand muscles.
Electroyte levels are eliminated, therefore cellular water is depleted.

stem: Who is held liable if an injury occurs as the result of an individual using a piece of equipment that is determined to be defective?
rationale: The manufacturer is held liable if the piece of equipment is determined to be defective or inadequate for its intended purpose.

stem: If assistant coach were to add hardware to a piece of equipment that is not part of the standard equipment, who is held liable?
rationale: The individual who modified the piece of equipment is liable; liability on part of the manufacturer is voided.

stem: What is the governing body that established voluntary test standards to reduce head injuries?
rationale: The National Operating Committee on Standards for Athletic Equipment (NOCSAE) has established minimum safety requirements for many sports that use equipment to protect the head.

stem: How often should NOCSAE-approved helmets be recertified/reconditioned?
rationale: A guideline for the reconditioning/recertifying helmets is every two years using a certified NOCSAE-approved vendor if no warranty exists or after the warranty expires.

stem: When fitting an athlete for protective headgear, what is a common guideline to follow for most helmets?
choices: Chin strap should be adjusted so that it gently contacts the chin when the mouth is closed.
The back of the helmet should match the length of the player’s hair.
The helmet should not move, or translate on the head if twisted or shaken.
It should contain the NOCSAE certification sticker on the outside of the helmet.
The more expensive, the more effective in preventing a head injury.
rationale: Guidelines discussed in the textbook for football, cycling, and ice hockey helmet fitting all inference that the helmet should not be loose or move around when the athlete moves the head.

stem: When properly fitting an ice hockey helmet, what is a correct procedure?
choices: Helmet should be loosely fitted around the ears.
Helmet should be three fingers width above the eyebrows.
Bottom of full cage face mask should rest gently on the chin.
Chin strap should be adjusted so that it gently contacts the chin when the mouth is closed.
Helmet should slightly touch the forehead, top, back, and sides of the head.
rationale: For hockey helmet fitting, the chin strap should be adjusted so that it gently contacts the chin when the mouth is closed, unlike the football chin strap that should assist in keeping the helmet from moving up and down or side to side.

stem: What sport has headgear that is a headband with a piece of foam in the front that is about 1 ½–2 inches wide?
rationale: To date, there is only one study that demonstrates that this headgear is effective in reducing the incidence of concussion or other head injuries, but a more recent systematic review demonstrated low evidence for reducing risks.

stem: What is an associated danger when a mouthpiece is cut down and only covers the front teeth?
choices: knocking out the front two teeth
rationale: A cut-down mouth guard can potentially become dislodged and lead to an obstructed airway.

stem: What phrase best completes this statement, “Eye protection must be worn by all athletes who play sports __________?
rationale: Eye protection must be worn by all athletes who play sports that use fast-moving projectiles. It is essential that athletes take special precautions to protect their eyes, with such as handball or racquetball.

stem: What is the suspensory ligament that can be stretched and cause premature sagging of the female breast if proper support equipment is not used?
rationale: Most sport bras are now designed to minimize excessive vertical and horizontal movements of the breasts that may stretch Cooper’s ligament causing premature sagging.

stem: What are the two key measurements that should be accounted for when fitting shoes?
choices: toe to end of shoe; full length of foot
heel to metatarsophalangeal joint; heel to end of the longest toe
heel to metatarsophalangeal joint; heel to end of the first toe
width of metatarsophalangeal arch; length of longitudinal arch
full length of foot; heel to first crease of the shoe
rationale: Two measurements must be considered when fitting shoes: 2. the distance from the heel to the metatarsophalangeal joint and 3. the distance from the heel to the end of the longest toe.

stem: Please check all of the boxes where the statement regarding shoes is correct.
choices: A tennis shoe should have no heel flare.
The flexibility in a running shoe should be at the ball of the foot.
An aerobic shoe should be flared for stability.
A sole or last of a tennis shoe should be made out of rubber.
The upper of a running shoe should be made out of leather with nylon.
The tread of a tennis shoe should be flattened.
There should be very little heel flare in an aerobic shoe.
Running shoes tread should have deep grooves for grip.
The heel and sole should be well padded in a tennis shoe similar to a running shoe.
The upper of an aerobic shoe should only be made of leather. K) The tread of an aerobic shoe should be flat or pivot dot. L) The sole of a tennis shoe should be firm.

stem: What statement regarding ankle braces is correct?
choices: Ankle braces have a significant effect on performance.
Research studies have proven that ankle braces are always more effective in preventing ankle injuries than ankle taping.
Research studies have proven that ankle taping is always more effective in preventing ankle injuries than ankle braces.
When compared to ankle taping, ankle braces have been proven to loosen more than ankle taping during physical activity.
Ankle braces probably have little to no effect on performance; any change is due to the athlete’s perception of support and comfort.
rationale: Ankle braces probably have little to no effect on performance, but are effective in preventing acute ankle sprains in most sports.

stem: What sport is wearing this piece of equipment a necessity in order to prevent injury?
rationale: Shin guards should be worn in soccer in order to prevent injuries to the shin or the lateral or medial tibia.

stem: A patient would wear this brace when they have suffered from what pathology?
rationale: Neoprene sleeves may be used to provide some support for patellofemoral conditions.

stem: What sport would wear this piece of equipment a necessity in order to prevent injury?
rationale: Protective gloves are essential in preventing injuries in sports like lacrosse and ice hockey.

stem: What related injuries can be prevented when using a properly fitted mouthguard?
A downhill skier is complaining about the glare on the snow. What color of tinted contact lens could be worn to reduce the glare?
rationale: Blue lenses can be used to assist in reducing the glare from the snow.

stem: Please check all of the face protection pieces that are utilized in soccer
What material combination for socks is less bulky and dries faster?
rationale: Cotton and polyester combination is less bulky and dries faster as opposed to socks made solely from cotton.

stem: Hydrophilic contact lenses are also known as __________
rationale: Athletes currently prefer the soft, hydrophilic lenses to the hard type.

stem: What type of knee brace is this? (Figure 7-29 b)
What muscle strain is being addressed in the video?
rationale: The video demonstrates a procedure used to support a hip flexor strain by helping lift the leg.

stem: When performing a hip spica wrapping technique, what is the most appropriate supply to use?
rationale: One 6-inch double-length elastic bandage and a roll of 1 ½-inch adhesive tape are used when performing a hip spica wrapping technique.

stem: When performing a shoulder spica, what statement is true?
choices: The bandage should be anchored around the upper arm on the unaffected side and then should be carried around the back.
The bandage should be anchored around the upper arm on the unaffected side and then should be carried around the front.
The bandage should be anchored around the upper arm on the affected side and then should be carried around the back.
The bandage should be anchored around the upper arm on the affected side and then should be carried around the front.
The bandage should be anchored around the chest and then wrapped around the affected arm.
rationale: When performing a shoulder spica, the bandage should be anchored around the upper arm on the affected side, and then carried around the back of the body.

stem: What is the purpose of the wrapping technique in the video?
choices: to minimally protect the ankle from injury
to secure a dressing in place
to hold a protective pad in place
to hold a compressive pad in place
rationale: The cloth ankle wrap is an inexpensive and expedient means of minimally protecting ankles from injury.

stem: The technique demonstrated in this diagram would be the most appropriate for which of the following injuries?
rationale: The sling and swathe combination is designed to stabilize the arm securely in cases of shoulder dislocation or fracture.

stem: Select only the applicable steps to be performed when applying a medial collateral ligament of the knee tape job.
choices: Apply anchor strips above and below the knee by lightly encircling the thigh and lower leg.
Apply a longitudinal strip laterally that runs the length to each anchor strip.
Apply a longitudinal strip medially that runs the length to each anchor strip.
Apply multiple, overlapping spiral strips that cross the knee joint.
Reverse the application of the spiral strips between superior and inferior.
Leave the patella exposed, free of tape.
Conclude the tape job with a patella-locking strip.
rationale: Basing these steps on Figure 8-3, they are applicable when addressing a medial, or lateral, collateral ligament sprain.

stem: What would be the most appropriate width of tape when taping a ballerina for a flexor hallicus longus muscle injury?
rationale: Fingers and toes usually require ½-inch or 1-inch tape; however, ½-inch tape would be the most appropriate based on the contours of the foot of a ballerina.

stem: What rule for tape application is correct?
choices: use continuous taping strips with non-elastic tape
tape all joints in full extension
allow tape to fit the natural contour of the skin
apply tape directly to the skin after applying a hot pack
rationale: Allowing tape to fit the natural contour of the skin is appropriate.

stem: What is being addressed in the video?
rationale: The X arch taping method is used to provide support to the longitudinal arch of the foot.

stem: What is being addressed in the video?
rationale: The LowDye Technique can be used for taping a plantar fascia strain, foot pronation, flattened medial longitudinal arch, arch strains, and plantar fasciitis.

stem: When performing a simple arch support taping technique, which statement is true?
choices: Begin at the navicular and pull tape medial to lateral applying 4–5 overlapping strips toward the toes
Begin at the navicular and pull tape lateral to medial applying 4–5 overlapping strips toward the toes
Begin just above the metatarsal arch and pull tape medial to lateral applying 4–5 overlapping strips toward the heel
Begin just above the metatarsal arch and pull tape lateral to medial applying 4–5 overlapping strips toward the heel
Begin at the navicular and continually pull tape in a circular motion around the entire foot applying 4–5 overlapping strips toward the toes
rationale: When performing a simple arch support, it should begin just above the metatarsal arch, then pulling the arch up by going lateral to medial with the tape strip applying 4–5 overlapping strips toward the heel.

stem: When applying tape for a knee hyperextension, the overlapping, multiple strips are layered in what format?
rationale: These four to five strips should form an X pattern with the center covering the popliteal space.

stem: What toe injury is being addressed in the video?
rationale: The greatest support is given to the joint by a half-figure-eight taping, also known as a spica technique.

stem: Click on the video for performing a closed basket weave (Gibney) technique to the ankle. At the ending of the video, what is the next step in the procedure?
rationale: After completing the conventional basket weave (using the Gibney strips), apply two or three heel locks to ensure maximum stability.

stem: What is the proper patient positioning for taping the Achilles tendon?
rationale: The patient kneels or lies face down with the affected foot hanging relaxed over the edge of the table.

stem: When taping for a medial collateral ligament knee sprain, how can it be ensured that the knee stays in a slightly flexed position?
choices: have the patient seated with leg hanging off edge of table
have the patient standing using a heel lift
have the patient place the heel of the injured leg on top of the uninjured foot
have the patient perform a ¼ squat
rationale: The patient stands on a 3-foot table with the injured knee held in a moderately relaxed position by a 1-inch heel lift.

stem: Which of the following is INCORRECTLY listed as a component of patellar orientation?
rationale: “Lift” is a technique used to correct a patellar tilt.

stem: What taping technique is being used in this video to tape for a sprained thumb?
rationale: After applying the anchor strips, three overlapping spica strips are applied around the metacarpophalangeal joint of the thumb.

stem: Where was the Kinesio taping technique developed?
rationale: It was developed in Japan and widely used in Europe and Asia.

stem: When taping for a bruised hand and using padding, what step should be performed to ensure the padding stays in place?
choices: repetitive encircling strips through the hand
X strips from the wrist to the metacarpals
1/2 inch tape strips laced through the webbing of the fingers that run to the wrist anchor strip
rationale: The laced strips that run through the webbing of the fingers help ensure the pad does not slip out and assists with the basic figure-eight wrap through the hand.

stem: What taping technique can be used to help provide additional protection for a sprained thumb?
rationale: Adding a checkrein strip(s) is applied by asking the patient to spread the injured fingers widely, but within a range that is free of pain. Then using a ½ inch adhesive tape apply around the middle phalanx of the injured and adjacent finger, then press the middle of the tape together and add a lock strip around the center that was pressed together.

stem: What is the premise of using Kinesio taping?
choices: It is therapeutic, activating neurological and circulatory systems with movement.
It is more economical than using traditional adhesive tapes.
It is less giving with greater tensile strength than traditional adhesive tapes.
It is more clinically evidence-based than traditional adhesive tapes.
rationale: While adhesive tape is structurally supportive, Kinesio tape is said to be therapeutic, activating neurological and circulatory systems with movement.

stem: When taping for plantar fasciitis using Kinesio taping techniques, how does the application of the longitudinal strips differ from the application of the traditional adhesive tapes?
choices: Application of the longitudinal strips runs transverse, rather than the length of the foot.
Application of the longitudinal strips is identical to the application of the traditional adhesive tapes.
Application of the longitudinal strips do not overlap, or criss-cross.
Application of the longitudinal strips borders only the outer perimeter of the foot.
Application of the longitudinal strips creates a basket-weave with the transverse strips.
rationale: Four pieces of 1-inch tape are extended over the bottom of the foot to the metatarsal heads while extending toes to stretch the plantar fascia.

stem: When taping for a low back strain using Kinesio taping techniques, what position should the patient be in when applying the three strips?
rationale: The patient is standing but bent forward to stretch the back muscles. The basic principle of Kinesio taping is to apply the tape from one end of a muscle to the other, with very little to no stretch on the tape from origin to insertion for muscle support and from insertion to origin during rehabilitation.

stem: Select only the CORRECT statements about the use of Kinesio tape.
choices: Muscle is usually placed in a shortened state in preparation for application of the Kinesio tape.
Kinesio tape has been supported in evidence-based research to change strength gains following application with healthy subject following a treatment.
Kinesio tape is latex-free and 100% cotton fabric with body heat.
There are no physical or chemical differences among the colors of Kinesio tape.
rationale: Muscle is usually placed on a gentle functional stretch with application of the tape at approximately 10% of its resting static length.

stem: What pathology of the shoulder is being addressed in the figure?
rationale: The placement of these particular Kinesio tape strips assists in providing stability to the shoulder.

stem: For an Achilles tendon taping, when wrapping the cut ends of the split longitudinal strip, where should these ends be placed?
choices: at the base of the foot
on both the medial and lateral aspect of the ankle
above the level of the strain
on top of the site of the strain
below the level of the strain
rationale: Caution is warranted to be sure to keep the wrapped ends of the split longitudinal strip above the level of the strain.

stem: What is the purpose of the open basket weave taping?
choices: It can be used for larger legs.
It is an alternate technique for an uninjured ankle.
It can be used for a combination of toe and ankle injury.
It can be used to control swelling.
It is an alternate technique for a syndesmotic ankle sprain.

stem: For the following muscular injuries, which one is characterized by an extremely painful involuntary muscle contraction that occurs more often in the calf, abdomen, or hamstrings?
rationale: Exercise-associate muscle cramps thought to occur most often in those muscle groups that are overloaded and fatigued during high-demand activities due to altered neuromuscular control rather than due to fluid and/or electrolyte imbalance as previously hypothesized.

stem: For the following muscular injuries, which one is characterized by a reflex reaction caused by trauma of the musculoskeletal system?
rationale: Muscle spasm is an involuntary muscle contraction that results in increased tension and shortening of that muscle or a group of muscles which interferes with voluntary movement. Two types of muscular spasms are clonic and tonic.

stem: What structure is the most commonly overused problem associated with activity?
rationale: Of all the overuse problems associated with activity, chronic overuse injuries involving a tendon are the most common representing approximately over 50% of all sports injuries.

stem: What is the name of a calcium formation in a large muscle due to repeated blows over the same area?
rationale: If the same area, or muscle, is bruised over and over again, a small calcium deposit may begin to accumulate in the injured area.

stem: What are the most significant risk factors for developing osteoarthritis?
rationale: Age and genetics are considered to be the most significant risk factors for developing osteoarthritis.

stem: What is classified as an example of an irregularly shaped bone?
rationale: Examples of irregularly shaped bones are in the vertebral column and the skull.

stem: What type of fracture occurs when an athlete is hit on one side of the head and a fracture occurs on the side opposite of the point where the trauma was initiated?
rationale: Contrecoup fractures occur on the side opposite to the point at which the trauma is initiated.

stem: What is neuropraxia?
choices: interruption in conduction of the impulse down the nerve fiber
numbness, prickling, or tingling of the nerve fiber
diminished sense of feeling of the nerve fiber
rationale: Neuropraxia is an interruption in conduction of the impulse down the axon due to demyelination. It is the mildest form of nerve injury.

stem: A patient is complaining of a diminished sense of feeling since sustaining an injury to the hand. When writing a SOAP note, what medical term would be used to describe this symptom?
rationale: Hypoesthesia is a sensory response that has results in a diminished sense of feeling.

stem: A cross-country runner is in the early stages of a stress fracture. How would the athlete describe the pain?
choices: There is pain when active but not at rest.
