What statement CORRECTLY states the basic principles for using PNF techniques?
choices: Visual cues should be implemented when performing PNF techniques.
Rotational movement is a component of PNF patterns.
Giving a quick stretch causes a reflex relaxation of that muscle.
When learning the patterns, the patient should avoid looking at the moving limb for biofeedback.
Pressing the joint together causes decreased stability.
rationale: Rotational movement is a critical component in all the PNF patterns.

stem: The technique demonstrated in this video should be utilized for what condition? problems?
rationale: Internal and external rotation would work on improving the rotator cuff muscles.

stem: This video demonstrates a technique that would be utilized for what patient situations?
choices: a patient that is progressively trying to regain strength through a range of motion
a patient that is focusing on coordination of the agonist and antagonist muscles
a patient that is trying to develop strength at a specific point in the range of movement
a patient that has maximum strength and is working on coordination
a patient that is trying to be functionally tested for return to play
rationale: Rhythmic initiation helps patients with limited movement progressively regain strength through the range of motion.

stem: How is the hold-relax stretching technique demonstrated in this video different from the contract-relax technique?
choices: The hold-relax uses an isokinetic contraction.
The hold-relax uses an isometric contraction.
The contract-relax uses an isometric contraction.
The contract-relax uses an isotonic contraction.
The contract-relax uses an isokinetic contraction.
rationale: The hold-relax technique is similar to the contract-relax except that an isometric contraction is used.

stem: What techniques does the PNF rhythmic stabilization exercise demonstrated in the video use?
choices: isometric contraction of the agonist and isotonic contraction of the antagonist
isotonic contraction of the agonist and isotonic contraction of the antagonist
isometric contraction of the agonist and isometric contraction of the antagonist
isokinetic contraction of the agonist and isometric contraction of the antagonist
isometric contraction of the agonist and isokinetic contraction of the antagonist
rationale: Rhythmic stabilization uses an isometric contraction of the agonists, followed by an isometric contraction of the antagonists.

stem: What PNF patterns is being demonstrated in the video?
choices: upper extremity D1 pattern moving into flexion
upper extremity D1 pattern moving into extension
upper extremity D2 pattern moving into flexion
upper extremity D2 pattern moving into extension
upper extremity D1 & D2 pattern moving into flexion
rationale: The video demonstrates upper extremity D1 pattern moving into flexion.

stem: What approach can the athletic trainer provide to ensure the patient’s adherence to the rehabilitation program?
choices: allow the patient to take time off at his/her discretion
be creative and allow patient to assist in designing rehabilitation program
ask that family and friends not join the patient while doing rehabilitation
encourage the patient that pain will be experienced
express from the start that sticking to the full program is very difficult
rationale: Creativity in designing and varying the exercise routine will help keep the patient interested and motivated.

stem: What statement from a patient’s progress note would disqualify a patient to be released back to activity?
choices: Patient reports pain scale of 0 out of 10 when performing all rehabilitation exercises.
Patient’s knee range of motion is bilaterally equal.
Patient reports of hesitance when not wearing prophylactic brace.
Patient reports that the athletic team is going full contact the week of release.
Patient’s girth measurement to the unaffected limb is ≤.25cm in comparison.
rationale: The sport-specific demand at the time of the patient’s release will involve full-contact, thus putting the patient at a greater risk of reinjury because of the demand of the activity.

stem: What scenario would be a contraindication for an athletic trainer to perform a joint mobilization technique?
choices: a golfer who has wrist pain after playing 18 holes
a swimmer who’s trapezius is in spasm
a javelin thrower who has multidirectional instability
a volleyball player who has had a capsular shrinkage
a football player who has had ligamentous reconstruction
rationale: Anyone who is suffering from joint instability should not have mobilizations performed on them.

stem: Who categorized mobilization techniques into five grades?
rationale: In Maitland’s system, grades I1 and II are used primarily to reat pin, and grades III and IV are used to treat stiffness.

stem: What grade of motion takes a patient’s joint past the point of limitation?
rationale: Grade 5 mobilizations are a manipulation where force is performed past the joint’s point of limitation.

stem: The technique demonstrated in the video can be classified as what technique?
rationale: The technique demonstrated is mobilization with manipulation for plantarflexion loss.

stem: What is another term for myofascial release?
rationale: Myofascial release has also been referred to as soft-tissue mobilizations.

stem: In order to perform the strain/counterstrain technique, what should be performed?
choices: Actively place the muscles in a position of greatest comfort.
Actively place the joint in neutral.
Actively place the muscles in a position of discomfort.
Passively place the muscles in a position of greatest comfort.
Passively place the muscles in a position of discomfort.
rationale: Strain/counterstrain is a passive technique that places the body in a position of greatest comfort, thereby relieving the pain.

stem: What muscle is the athletic trainer addressing while performing a positional release?
rationale: The video demonstrates a positional release of the quadriceps.

stem: What muscle is the athletic trainer addressing while performing a positional release?
rationale: The video demonstrates a positional release of the hip adductors.

stem: What muscle is the athletic trainer addressing while performing a positional release?
rationale: The video demonstrates a positional release of the levator scapula.

stem: What soft tissue mobilization technique uses hand-held stainless steel instruments designed to scan and treat injured tissue using specific strokes?
rationale: Graston Technique® is an advanced method of instrument-assisted soft tissue mobilization that uses six different sized instruments with specific strokes along with a comprehensive treatment approach.

stem: What treatment technique is designed to identify and correct asymmetrical postural patterns?
rationale: Postural restoration treatment focuses on asymmetrical patterns and multiple joint muscles in combination with diaphragmatic breathing.

stem: What is the purpose of using biofeedback?
choices: to allow the athletic trainer to measure muscle strength
to focus on the patient’s ability to increase involuntary muscle strength
to provide to the physician measurable outcomes for return to activity
to help a patient develop greater voluntary muscular control

stem: What is the method by which drugs are absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and eliminated or excreted by the body?
rationale: Pharmacokinetics is the method by which drugs are absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and eliminated or excreted by the body.

stem: If a patient is suffering from asthma, what is the ideal method to administer a daily medication?
rationale: Inhalation is a means of bringing medication or substances to the respiratory tract. This method is most often used in sports to relieve the athlete of the symptoms of respiratory illnesses such as asthma.

stem: What is the most common method of administering medicine?
rationale: Oral administration of medicine is the most common method of drug administration.

stem: What method of drug administration is given via an injection and is rapidly spread throughout the body?
rationale: Intravenous is an injection given into the vein when an immediate reaction to the medication is desired.

stem: Alcohol is an example of what external administration method for medications?
rationale: Solutions can be administered externally and examples include antiseptics, disinfectants, vasoconstrictors, and liquid rubefacients (alcohol, turpentine).

stem: How often a pain reliever is taken is dependent upon what?
rationale: The half-life is critical in determining how often and in what dosage a drug must be administered to achieve and maintain therapeutic levels of concentration.

stem: What statements are correct in regard to agencies and regulations that govern the provision of pharmaceutical care?
choices: The Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act of 1938 regulates the quality, strength, and labeling of prescription and non-prescription drugs.
The Federal Anti-Tampering Act of 1983 mandates tamper-resistant packaging on all prescription drugs.
The Federal Controlled Substances Act of 1970 separates prescription from non- prescription drugs.
The Durham-Humphrey Amendment of 1951 mandates repackaging of medications.
The Anti-Drug Abuse Act of 1988 regulates anabolic steroids as controlled substances.
Health Insurance Portability-Accountability Act of 1996 provides national standards to protect the privacy of personal health information.

stem: What statement goes against the general guidelines for administering medications?
choices: Make sure the patient reads the label information prior to taking the medication.
Make sure the patient knows the dosage schedule for the medications he or she is taking.
The containers do not need to be childproof if the patient is over 18 years of age.
Provide both verbal and written instructions for the use of medications.
Advise patients not to share medications with other people.
rationale: All containers need to be childproof no matter what the age of the patient.

stem: What items should an athletic trainer record on a medication administration log?

An athlete claims ibuprofen is needed prior to each practice in order to complete the whole practice. The athlete had been taking ibuprofen for a lateral ankle sprain that occurred a month earlier; however, the physician has instructed the athlete to cease taking medications due to lack of swelling and pain. What term would best describe the athlete’s general response produced by the ibuprofen?
rationale: Habituation is an individual’s development of a psychological need for a specific medication.

stem: If a person has a headache and takes acetaminophen and the headache dissipates, what term would best describe the body’s response produced by taking the drug?
rationale: Specific effect is an action usually produced by a drug in a select tissue or organ system.

stem: What antibiotic is most often used for streptococcal infection and mycoplasma pneumoniae?
rationale: Macrolides, such as erythromycin and azithromycin, are most often used for streptococcal infection and mycoplasma pneumoniae.

stem: Why would a patient take an antipruritic drug?
rationale: Antipruritics relieve itching.

stem: What would be classified as a narcotic analgesic drug?
rationale: Codeine resembles morphine in its action but is less potent. It is classified as a narcotic analgesic.

stem: What value would be considered the maximum daily dose for ibuprofen?
rationale: 3200 mg is the maximum daily dose of ibuprofen that an individual should ingest.

stem: What statement is a contraindication regarding prolonged use of cortisone?
rationale: Prolonged use of corticosteroids can produce dermatological problems. An example is delayed wound healing.

stem: If a patient is suffering from flatulence, what should be taken?
rationale: Carminatives are drugs that give relief from flatulence.

stem: What type of drugs can decrease an athlete’s ability to sweat, therefore predisposing them to a heat illness?
rationale: Antihistamines can decrease the peripheral mechanisms of sweating that impair the body’s ability to dissipate heat and thus predispose the athlete to heat-related illness.

stem: What is the medicinal use of the apparatus illustrated in the picture?
choices: It is a metered-dose inhaler used to treat asthma.
It is a proton pump inhibitor used to treat GERD.
It is a glucose blood meter used in the maintenance of diabetes.
It is an epinephrine pen used to treat anaphylaxis.
It is a syringe injector used to administer vitamin B12.
rationale: An EpiPen can be used to treat severe allergic reactions.

stem: If a SOAP note history reported that a patient was currently taking Coumadin, what is its significance?
choices: There is no significance, nor contraindications for this type of medication.
This medication is an anticoagulant that slows clotting time of blood.
This is an antihistamine and may cause drowsiness.
This is an ergogenic aid and should be reported as an NCAA infraction.
This is an over-the-counter acetylsalicylic acid that should not be combined with acetaminophen.
rationale: The most common anticoagulants used by physicians are heparin and coumarin.

stem: Why would diuretics increase the rate of heat illness?
choices: They decrease the peripheral mechanism of sweating.
They prevent volume expansion and limit cutaneous vasodilation.
They affect hypothalamic heat control and have anticholinergic activity.
They both affect cold and hold temperature regulation.
rationale: Diurectics, such as furosemide and bumetanide, can prevent volume expansion and limit cutaneous vasodilation.

stem: Please check all of the indicators that would help in identifying an individual who is abusing a substance.
Please check all of the drugs that are considered opioids.
Within the two major categories of stimulants, which is legal to obtain, or with a prescription, and commonly used for asthmatics?
rationale: B2 agonists are prescription-level drugs used to treat and/or manage asthma.

stem: What ergogenic aid increases blood volume and the number of blood cells?
rationale: Blood reinjection, aka blood doping, blood packing, blood boosting, is the removal of 900 milliliters of blood, storing it, and reinfusing it after six weeks.

stem: Please check all that apply as they relate to the long-term effects of cocaine use:
What recreational drug should be EXCLUDED as one of the most abused?
rationale: Although amphetamines are an abused drug that overlaps with those thought to enhance performance, it does not fit within the list of recreational drugs.

stem: What organization was given full authority to develop programs, policies, and procedures for Olympic, Pan American, and Paralympic sports in the United States?
choices: The National Federation of State High School Associations (NFHS)
rationale: The United States Anti-Doping Agency (USADA) was given full authority to execute a comprehensive national anti-doping program encompassing testing, adjudication, education, and research and to develop programs, policies, and procedures for Olympic, Pan American, and Paralympic sports in the United States.

stem: If a suspected drug overdose has occurred, which would be an essential professional contact be immediately notified?
rationale: Each state has at least one. The experts at the poison control center will provide instructions for immediate care of the individual until the rescue squad arrives.

stem: In the process of drug-testing for the NCAA or USOC, what is the purpose of taking two specimens (A and B), or urine samples?
choices: The second specimen is what is sent to the laboratory to be tested for traces of drugs.
The second specimen is held in case the specimen A tests positive.
The second specimen is used to do a urinalysis for quality and ownership of the urine samples provided.
The second specimen is used in case the first sample’s quality is compromised.
The second specimen is collected so randomization may occur between the samples when being tested.
rationale: The second specimen (B) is used only when a reconfirmation is needed for a positive test of specimen A.

stem: What drug can increase the rate of heat illness?
rationale: Diuretics can increase the rate of heat illness. They can prevent volume expansion and limit cutaneous vasodilation.

stem: What statement is true about disinfectants?
choices: They are substances that kill or inhibit the growth of microorganisms.
They are substances made from the alkaloid of opium.
They are stored throughout the body and in the brain tissues for weeks and even months.
They tend to make arterial walls more permeable to fatty substances.
They suppress the formation of prothrombin in the liver.
rationale: Disinfectants are substances that combat microorganisms but should be applied only to nonliving objects.

stem: What does the medication Coumadin do?
choices: It is used to treat exercise-induced bronchospasm.
It is used to slow clotting time in certain vascular disorders.
It is used to increase the production of fluid in the respiratory system.
It is used to control diarrhea.
It is used to treat anaphylaxis resulting from insect stings.
rationale: Coumadin is used to slow clotting time in certain vascular disorders. It acts by suppressing the formation of prothrombin in the liver.

stem: What statement is true about dry powder inhalers?
choices: They can be used with a spacer that keeps medication from escaping into the air.
They convert the medication into a fine powder because of the pressure within the canister.
They require an individual to place the lips on their mouthpiece and inhale rapidly.
They convert medication into a mist.
rationale: Dry powder inhalers are not pressurized but rather release medication when the patient rapidly inhales. This type of inhaler requires the individual to place the lips on the mouthpiece and inhale more rapidly than with a traditional metered-dose inhaler and tend to be easier to use than a metered-dose inhaler.

stem: What type of inhaler is being demonstrated in the figure?
rationale: A metered-dose inhaler is commonly used by patients who have asthma. A metered-dose inhaler includes a pressurized canister with measured doses of medication inside.

stem: What substance is approved by the U.S. Olympic Committee (USOC) to be used by asthmatics who develop exercise-induced bronchospasms?
rationale: The U.S. Olympic Committee (USOC) has approved some substances to be used by asthmatics who develop exercise-induced bronchospasms. These substances are selective B2 agonists, consisting of albuterol (Proventil), salmeterol (Serevent), and formoterol (Foradil).

stem: What ergogenic aid is produced by the somatotropic cells of the anterior region of the pituitary gland, from which it is released into the circulatory system?
rationale: Human growth hormone (HGH) is produced by the somatotropic cells of the anterior region of the pituitary gland, from which it is released into the circulatory system. The amount released varies with age and the developmental periods of a person’s life.

stem: What ergogenic aid is used in humans to produce transient increases in testosterone in males and particularly in females primarily for the purpose of enhancing athletic performance?
rationale: Androstenedione is a relatively weak androgen that is produced primarily in the testes and in lower amounts by the adrenal cortex and ovaries and is classified as a dietary supplement. It has been used in humans to produce transient increases in testosterone in males and particularly in females primarily for the purpose of enhancing athletic performance.

stem: What statement is true about smokeless tobacco?
choices: It can cause oral and throat cancer.
It is a less addictive habit than smoking.
It increases total response time among athletes and nonathletes.
Historically, its use can be attributed primarily to asthmatic athletes.
Its user is exposed to the tar and carbon monoxide associated with a burning cigarette.
rationale: Smokeless tobacco causes bad breath, stained teeth, tooth sensitivity to heat and cold, cavities, gum recession, tooth bone loss, leukoplakia, and oral and throat cancer.

stem: What is the name of the act that provides legal protection to the team physician and athletic trainers who provide healthcare for athletes when traveling to other states?
rationale: The Sports Medicine Clarity Act was passed to provide legal protection to the team physician and athletic trainers who provide healthcare for their athletes when traveling to other states.

stem: Who can never dispense or administer any type of medication to a patient?
rationale: Unlicensed students may never dispense or administer any type of medication to a patient.

stem: Why should a written formulary be executed before traveling out of state?
choices: It creates a modified prescription for on-the-go administration.
It allows the athletic trainer to travel across state lines with prescriptive medications.
It is list of all medications and their use signed by a team physician.
It permits the athletic trainer to dispense any form of medication as needed.
It allows both the athletic trainer and physician to share medications within the athletic team.
rationale: Prior to travel, the athletic trainer should prepare a written formulary, which is a list that is signed by the team physician that has all the medication names, indications for use, and dose instructions along with the contact information for the team physician.

stem: Coffee, a one cup measurement, contains 100 mg of caffeine. A 5 Hour Energy (2oz) contains how many times of that amount?
What action is permitted by the metatarsophalangeal joints?
rationale: The metatarsophalangeal joints permit hinge action of the phalanges. This action is similar to the action between the hand and the fingers.

stem: What structure in the foot functions to assist in reducing pressure in weight bearing?
rationale: Two sesamoid bones are located beneath the 1stfirst MTP joint to increase the mechanical advantage of the flexor tendons of the great toe and to act as sliding pulleys for tendons.

stem: Why is full dorsiflexion the most stable position of the talocrural joint?
choices: because the talus is broader anteriorly than posteriorly
because the subtalar joint limits dorsiflexion
because the talus is narrower anteriorly than posteriorly
because the calcaneus is wider posteriorly
because the calcaneus is narrower posteriorly
rationale: Plantar flexion relies most heavily on the ligaments for stability because the narrower posterior portion of the talus does not provide adequate surface area and contour for bony congruency. Dorsiflexion is the most stable position of the talocrural joint primarily due to the bony congruency between the talus and the mortise.

stem: What is its average range of motion for dorsiflexion at the talocrural joint?
rationale: The average range of motion is 10 degrees for dorsiflexion and 23 degrees in for plantar flexion. Because the talus fits principally into the space formed by the malleoli, lateral movement is restricted by the stabilizing ligaments of the ankle.

stem: What structure supports the foot against downward forces?
rationale: Along with ligaments, the plantar fascia supports the foot against downward forces. The plantar fascia is a thick, white band of fibrous tissue originating from the medial tuberosity of the calcaneus and ending at the proximal heads of the metatarsals.

stem: What ligament connects the shafts of the metatarsals?
rationale: Shafts of the metatarsals are connected by interosseous ligaments. The bases are connected by plantar and dorsal ligaments, and the heads are attached by transverse metatarsal ligaments.

stem: What joint consists of the calcaneocuboid and the talonavicular joints?
rationale: The midtarsal joint, also referred to as Chopart’s joint, consists of two distinct joints: the calcaneocuboid and the talonavicular joint. The midtarsal joint depends mainly on ligamentous and muscular tension to maintain position and integrity.

stem: Which articulation of the foot is known as Lisfranc’s joint?
rationale: Dorsal displacement of the proximal end of the metatarsals defines a Lisfranc injury.

stem: What movement occurs at the subtalar joint?
rationale: Inversion and eversion refer to a movement of the calcaneus, and pronation and supination refer to multiplanar movements that occur during weight bearing.

stem: What motion would be unlikely to occur at the subtalar joint?
rationale: The subtalar joint is the articulation between the talus and the calcaneus in which inversion, eversion, pronation, and supination are normal movements that occur.

stem: What statement is TRUE regarding gait?
choices: There is no non-weight-bearing period or phase with a walking gait.
At midstance and terminal stance, the body is supported by both limbs at initial contact of a walking gait.
In running, the stance phase accounts for one-quarter of the gait cycle.
In running, there is a time when neither foot is in contact with the ground and no time when both feet contact the ground simultaneously.
In the running gait, initial contact of the foot usually occurs on the medial aspect of the calcaneus.
rationale: In the running gait, there is a period after toe-off in which neither foot is in contact with the ground; in running, stance phase accounts for only one-third of the gait cycle.

stem: What is a compensating action at the subtalar joint when structural or functional deformities exist in the foot or leg?
choices: Excessive pronation during the stance phase will cause the midtarsal joint to remain unlocked, resulting in an excessively loose foot.
At heel strike in prolonged or excessive supination, the midtarsal joint will lock, causing the foot to remain excessively loose.
Excessive supination limits tibial external rotation.
Prolonged pronation will not allow the foot to resupinate in time to provide a rigid lever for heel strike.
In the pronated position, the peroneus longus tendon loses its ability to stabilize the first ray, making it hypomobile.
rationale: Excessive pronation during the stance phase will cause this compensatory subtalar joint motion; as more motion occurs, the first metatarsal and first cuneiform will become more mobile.

stem: The body is supported by a single limb in which period of the stance phase of a normal gait cycle?
rationale: At midstance and terminal stance in the stance phase of a normal gait cycle, the body is supported by a single limb. It needs the support of both the legs in the early portion of the loading response period and at initial contact.

stem: What would be considered an ideal shoe for the pronated foot?
choices: less flexible with good rearfoot control
more flexible with a medial wedge
less flexible with a lateral wedge
more flexible with good rearfoot control
less flexible with a wider toebox
rationale: Pronation is a problem of hypermobility. Those who do pronate need stability and firmness to reduce this excess movement.

stem: In the observation section of a foot assessment, what would be considered an inappropriate action to perform?
choices: assessing if the foot changes color when weight bearing and non-weight bearing
assessing for deformity, swelling, or discoloration
rationale: Assessing the patient’s pain level, although can be an objective measurement, should be asked during the history or subjective section of the assessment.

stem: What position should the foot/ankle be in when observing for structural deformities?
rationale: The position at which the talus is equally prominent on both sides is considered a neutral subtalar position in which the subtalar joint is neither pronated nor supinated.

stem: What would be unable to be discovered during the palpation section of a foot assessment?
rationale: Ligamentous integrity is part of the special tests section of a foot assessment where stress is applied to the structure.

stem: What injury to the tarsal region commonly occurs from landing after a jump or due to a fall from a height?
rationale: A fracture of the calcaneus most often occurs from landing after a jump or fall from a height. Avulsion fractures can also occur anteriorly at the attachment of the calcaneonavicular ligament to the sustentaculum tali or posteriorly at the attachment of the talocalcaneal ligament.

stem: Of the injuries that can be sustained in the tarsal region of the foot, what has an etiology of a severe inversion and dorsiflexion force, or medially from an inversion and plantar flexion force with external rotation of the tibia on the talus?
rationale: This particular mechanism, or etiology, can result in a fracture to the dome of the talus.

stem: If a patient is suffering from metatarsalgia, what would be a symptom?
choices: pain in the ball of the foot
pain in the medial arch of the foot
pain at the heel of the foot
rationale: A person suffering from metatarsalgia will have pain in the ball of the foot. This is often a complication of a Lisfranc injury.

stem: What is the most common location for a metatarsal stress fracture?
rationale: The most common metatarsal stress fracture in the foot involves the shaft of the second metatarsal and is often referred to as a march fracture.

stem: What is the cause of a turf toe injury?
rationale: Hyperextension of the great toe results in a sprain of the metatarsophalangeal joint.

stem: What injury causes pain at the posterior heel below the attachment of the Achilles tendon insertion?
rationale: In Sever's disease, pain occurs at the posterior heel below the attachment of the Achilles tendon insertion of a child or adolescent athlete. Pain occurs during vigorous activity and does not continue at rest.

stem: What is often a complication of retrocalcaneal bursitis?
rationale: Exostoses is a benign bony outgrowth that protrudes from the surface of a bone and is usually capped by cartilage. Because of the ongoing inflammation of the retrocalcaneal bursa, it is sometimes referred to as a pump bump.

stem: What is a sign or symptom of pes planus?
choices: heavy callus development on the ball of the foot
heavy callus development of the heel of the foot
rationale: Bulging of the navicular bone is a sign that a person may be suffering from pes planus.

stem: What condition is illustrated in this figure?
rationale: Hallux valgus deformity is a bunion that occurs at the head of the first metatarsal.

stem: This type of pad (see below) can be used to treat all of the following problems except for which condition?
rationale: Morton’s neuroma is treated by a teardrop pad not a doughnut pad.

stem: The technique demonstrated in the video would be utilized for what problem?
rationale: Dramatic treatment results may be obtained by manipulating to restore the cuboid to its natural position.

stem: The exercise demonstrated in this video would be best suited for a person suffering from what foot condition?
rationale: Individuals who suffer from pes cavus may receive relief by stretching the Achilles tendon.

stem: This special test will help identify a patient who is suffering from what condition?
rationale: Morton’s neuroma can be exacerbated by squeezing the metatarsal heads together.

stem: What is a painful condition caused by the proliferation of bony spurs on the dorsal aspect of the first metatarsophalangeal joint?
rationale: Hallux rigidus is a painful condition caused by the proliferation of bony spurs on the dorsal aspect of the first metatarsophalangeal joint.

stem: What would cause a subungual hematoma?
rationale: Kicking an object can cause a subungual hematoma located under the nail bed.

stem: What joint mobilization techniques decrease hypomobility of the metatarsals?
choices: anterior and posterior cuboid metatarsal glides
rationale: Anterior and posterior tarsometatarsal glides decrease hypomobility of the metatarsals. They are some of the many manual joint mobilization techniques that are useful in maintaining or normalizing joint motions.

stem: The video is demonstrating anterior/posterior talonavicular glides. Why would this joint mobilization be used?
rationale: Anterior/posterior talonavicular glides are used for increasing adduction and abduction.

stem: What component of rehabilitation is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: Gathering towel exercises can be done in order to work on strengthening the foot.

stem: What component of rehabilitation is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: Gripping and spreading the toes can be done in order to work on strengthening the foot.

stem: What component of rehabilitation is demonstrated in the video?
rationale: Walking on balance shoes can be done in order to work on regaining neuromuscular control of the foot.

stem: Please check all of the activities that are classified as functional activities of the foot.
Select only the body parts that make up the passive subsystem of the foot core system.
choices: ligaments plus bony congruency of the joints
rationale: Muscles are the active subsystem and the neural subsystem is comprised of both subsystems.

stem: Select only the tarsal bones that articulate with at least five or more bones.
rationale: The calcaneus articulates with two bones: talus and cuboid. The talus articulates with four bones: tibia, fibula, navicular, and calcaneus. The medial cuneiform articulates with only the intermediate cuneiform, first metatarsal, and the navicular.

stem: What is condition UNRELATED to a contributing cause for tarsal tunnel syndrome?
rationale: Any condition that compromises the structures within the tunnel can cause tarsal tunnel syndrome, including tenosynovitis, previous fractures, excessive pronation, and any acute trauma.

stem: What foot injury has a high nonunion rate?
rationale: Healing of a Jones fracture is slow and frustrating for the patient. This injury has a high nonunion rate, and the course of healing is unpredictable.

stem: What pathology would benefit from using a night splint?
Why is the lower third of this bone the most common site for fractures of the leg?
choices: It does not have any muscular coverage and is vulnerable to outside trauma.
It is the most pronounced change in structure, producing an anatomical weakness.
It is common insertion point for the lower leg muscles predisposing it to stress fractures.
It is a common point of origin for the lower leg muscles predisposing it to stress fractures.
It is where the interosseous membrane is the weakest.
rationale: The tibia is triangularly shaped in its upper two-thirds but is rounded and more constricted in the lower third.

stem: What is the name of the joint that is formed with the inferior portions of the tibia and fibula?
rationale: The inferior tibiofibular joint is a fibrous articulation, called the syndesmosis, the site of a high- ankle sprain.

stem: What are the movements that occur at the talocrural joint?
rationale: The talocrural joint, or ankle mortise, allows plantar flexion and dorsiflexion.

stem: Which ligament is the primary resistance to foot eversion?
rationale: The deltoid ligament is comprised of four ligaments that create a triangular ligament group and along with the spring ligament helps to maintain the inner longitudinal arch.

stem: What is the primary function of the calcaneofibular ligament?
choices: restrains the anterior displacement of the talus
restrains the posterior displacement of the talus
prevents abduction and eversion of ankle and subtalar joint
prevents eversion, pronation, and anterior displacement of the talus
rationale: The calcaneofibular ligament is usually involved with a grade 2 inversion ankle sprain.

stem: What structures help prevent medial or lateral displacement of the talus?
rationale: Even though the talus is anatomically designed to contribute ankle stability, the malleoli prevent medial or lateral displacement.

stem: Please check all of the muscles that invert the foot.
In order to prevent injury to the Achilles complex, it is recommended to stretch with both the knee extended and with it flexed 15-30 degrees. Why?
choices: The knee needs to be extended and bent to allow the superficial and deep aspect of the Achilles tendon to be stretched.
The knee needs to be extended and bent to allow the superficial and deep compartments of the lower leg to be stretched.
The knee needs to be extended and bent to allow both static and dynamic stretching of the involved muscle groups.
The knee needs to be extended and bent to allow full dorsiflexion at the ankle.
The knee needs to be extended and bent to allow stretching of both the gastrocnemius and the soleus.
rationale: The superficial posterior compartment of the lower leg is comprised of the gastrocnemius and soleus muscle. Both have varying origin sites that require an extended (gastrocnemius) and bent (soleus) position of the knee.

stem: From the following, which one would be considered an INCORRECT approach in the prevention of ankle sprains?
choices: stretching of the tibialis anterior muscle
rationale: Stretching of the Achilles tendon is a key step in the prevention of ankle sprains so that full ankle dorsiflexion can be achieved.

stem: What would be considered a key element to address with patients who have chronic ankle instabilities?
rationale: Individuals with chronic ankle instability must develop neuromuscular control at the ankle joint.

stem: In the history section of an ankle assessment, what would be considered an INAPPROPRIATE question to ask the patient?
choices: Have you ever hurt your ankle before?
Do you think your age contributes to your chronic ankle sprains?
What first aid and therapy, if any, were given for this, or previous injuries?
Was there immediate swelling, or was it gradual, or none at all?
rationale: Age would only be an appropriate question when identifying demographics of the patient and growth plate disorders.

stem: In the observation section of an ankle assessment, what would be considered an INAPPROPRIATE action to perform?
choices: assessing the width of the hips
assessing if there is crepitus or abnormal sounds in the ankle joint
assessing if patient is in obvious pain
assessing for deformity, swelling, or discoloration
rationale: A more appropriate physical assessment would be to perform a postural assessment looking for deviations.

stem: If weight-bearing for the patient were tolerable, what would be an INAPPROPRIATE patient observation for structural deformities of the ankle / lower leg?
rationale: The other listed patient positions are appropriate since the patient is weight-bearing tolerable, but if a fracture is suspected running would not be correct.

stem: What would be unable to be discovered during the palpation section of an ankle assessment?
rationale: A retinaculum tear is difficult to palpate for and is usually discovered during the special tests section, or during a manual muscle test.

stem: What is known as a bimalleolar fracture?
rationale: An inversion fracture of sufficient magnitude could cause both an avulsion fracture of the lateral malleolus and a fracture of the medial malleolus.

stem: Please check all of the statements that appropriately apply to the Ottawa ankle rules when determining the need of a radiograph.
choices: inability to bear weight (4 steps taken independently, even if limping) at time of injury and time of evaluation
tenderness over superior or anterior pole of either malleolus
inability to bear weight for 4 steps (2 on each foot) at the time of injury and time of evaluation
tenderness along the base of the1stFirst metatarsal or navicular bone
tenderness over the inferior or posterior pole of either malleolus, including the distal 6 cm

stem: Of the varying ankle sprains that can occur, which one has an etiology of moderate force on the ankle while it is in a position of inversion, plantar flexion, and/or adduction?
rationale: A grade 2 ankle sprain has a high incidence among active individuals and causes a great deal of disability with many days of lost time.

stem: Of the varying ankle sprains that can occur, which one has an etiology of increased external rotation or forced dorsiflexion?
rationale: A syndesmotic ankle sprain often occurs in conjunction with a severe sprain of the medial and lateral ligament complexes.

stem: Avulsion fractures are common in which of the following?
choices: grade 2 & 3 inversion or eversion ankle sprains
rationale: Avulsion fractures are common in grade 2 and grade 3 eversion or inversion ankle sprains.

stem: A person is suffering from a grade 2 lateral ankle sprain. When should the activity demonstrated in the video begin?
choices: during the remodeling tissue healing phase
during the repair tissue healing phase
it should be done when determining return to play
it should begin in the acute injury tissue healing phase
rationale: Toe raises should begin during Phase 2 or the repair tissue healing phase.

stem: The technique demonstrated in the video is used to diagnose what?
rationale: The Thompson test is used to determine if an individual has suffered a rupture of the Achilles tendon.

stem: The technique demonstrated in the video can best be used to manage what condition? What?
rationale: A conservative approach when dealing with a peroneal tendon subluxation/dislocation is compression with a pad cut in a horseshoe-shaped pattern that surrounds the lateral malleolus.

stem: If a person is suffering from posterior tibialis tendinitis, where would it be point tender?
choices: the base of the fifth metatarsal
rationale: Point tenderness and edema will be located directly behind the medial malleolus.

stem: What is the main purpose of the pad in this picture?
choices: provide support to the lateral ankle
provide stability for a bone fracture
provide support to the medial ankle
rationale: A horseshoe-shaped pad placed around the malleolus provides excellent focal compression when held in place by an elastic wrap.

stem: A patient is suffering from medial tibial stress syndrome, complaining of pain before and after activity, but it does not affect performance. What grade of pain would the patient be assigned?
rationale: Grade 2 pain is classified as pain occurring before and after activity but not affecting performance.

stem: What diagnostic technique would be the MOST APPROPRIATE for diagnosis of a tibial stress fracture?
rationale: A bone scan will accurately assess the presence of a stress fracture.

stem: The BAPS board exercises demonstrated in the video would be classified as what?
rationale: The BAPS board is a method of increasing neuromuscular control or balance.

stem: The activity demonstrated in this video would be MOST APPROPRIATE to functionally test who?
choices: a hockey player who suffered a shin contusion
a sprinter who suffered a stress fracture
a wide receiver who suffered a lateral ankle sprain
a baseball catcher who suffered from acute compartment syndrome
a volleyball player who suffered from a calf cramp
rationale: The speed ladder in-and-out drill closely mimics the activities that a wide receiver would perform during competition. The drill also places stress on the lateral structures of the ankle.

Please match the name of the special test with the video.
