Which of the following injuries is managed with the use of immobilization with a figure- eight brace?
rationale: A clavicular fracture can be stabilized by applying a sling and swathe bandage and by treating the patient for possible shock.

stem: What additional structure could be compromised with a fracture of the humerus?
rationale: The radial nerve encircles the humeral shaft and could be severed by jagged bone edges resulting in radial nerve paralysis, causing wrist drop and the inability to perform forearm supination.

stem: Which of the following injuries has an etiology of indirect force transmitted through the humerus of the shoulder joint by direct violence such as a blow that strikes a poorly padded clavicle?
rationale: Depending on the direction of force, the medial end of the clavicle can be displaced upward and forward, slightly anteriorly.

stem: If a patient exhibits posterior separation of the clavicle with complete disruption of the acromioclavicular ligament, what grade sprain was sustained?
rationale: A grade 4 acromioclavicular ligament sprain is characterized by posterior separation of the clavicle with complete disruption of the acromioclavicular ligament.

stem: Which of the following statements is true of a Bankart lesion?
choices: It is an anterior defect on the labrum.
It is a defect found on the posterior lateral aspect of the humeral head.
It is caused by the compression of the cancellous bone of the head of the humerus against the anterior glenoid rim.
It is caused by an injury to the superior aspect of the labrum.
It is created by a divot in the humeral head.
rationale: A detached labrum and capsule can produce a permanent anterior defect on the labrum called a Bankart lesion. A moderately frequent occurrence resulting from instability in primary and recurrent dislocations would be a bony Bankart lesion.

stem: What type of shoulder condition affects the attachment of the long head of the biceps to the superior labrum?
rationale: A SLAP lesion can affects the attachment of the long head of the biceps to the superior labrum.

stem: The acronym GIRD stands for which of the following?
rationale: GIRD is often seen in swimmers and throwers and stands for glenohumeral internal rotation deficit.

stem: If a patient were suffering from thoracic outlet syndrome, which of the following symptoms is not inapplicable?
rationale: A person who has thoracic outlet syndrome would suffer from muscle atrophy, not muscle hypertrophy.

stem: Upon viewing an MRI it is noted that the patient has an anterior defect on the labrum. What is this defect called?
rationale: A Bankart lesion is usually slow, and the detached labrum and capsule can produce a permanent anterior defect on the labrum.

stem: What muscle or muscle group is responsible for decelerating the humerus in the deceleration phase of throwing?
choices: internal rotators of the rotator cuff
external rotators of the rotator cuff
rationale: The external rotators of the rotator cuff contract eccentrically during the deceleration phase to decelerate the humerus.

stem: During what phase of throwing are both shoulders adducted, externally rotated and horizontally abducted?
rationale: During the windup phase of throwing both shoulders are adducted, externally rotated and horizontally abducted.

stem: What is the clinical premise for completing a Subjective Shoulder Scale Assessment?
choices: to establish patient quality of dermatomes
to establish patient range of motion
to establish patient efficiency of myotomes
to establish patient pain and function
to establish patient mental and emotional status
rationale: This assessment is both patient-and physician-derived and used for outcomes assessment in patients with shoulder instability, rotator cuff disease, and glenohumeral arthritis.

stem: What direction is the acromion process forced with an acromioclavicular joint sprain?
rationale: The acromion process is forced downward, backward and inward with an acromioclavicular joint sprain.

stem: A baseball pitcher suffered an anterior shoulder subluxation last season and is returning as the starting pitcher this season. During what phase of throwing would concerned be the greatest of a re-injury?
rationale: This is the phase when maximum external rotation occurs.

stem: What is the name of the exercise for the shoulder being performed in the video?
rationale: Codman’s pendulums are being demonstrated and may begin immediately after injury to work on range of motion.

stem: The exercise being performed in the video is good for increasing what motion(s) of the glenohumeral joint?
rationale: Shoulder flexion and abduction can be increased by performing wall climbs or rope-and-pulley exercises.

stem: The exercise being performed in the video is best for strengthening which of the following shoulder complex structures?
rationale: Y, W, T exercises on a physioball are best for strengthening scapular stabilizers.

stem: The exercise being performed in the video is best for strengthening which of the following shoulder girdle muscles?
rationale: A bench press is done to strengthen the pectoralis major and triceps.

stem: Which shoulder joint movement is described as a downward movement of the humerus toward the midline of the body in the plane of the scapula?
rationale: Shoulder adduction is moving the humerus toward the midline of the body in the scapular plane.

stem: What special test is being demonstrate in this picture?
rationale: The sulcus test is used to check for multidirectional instability.

stem: What does this special test check for?
rationale: The lift off test is positive if a patient is unable to lift the hand posteriorly off the back. This is positive for a subscapularis tear.

stem: What muscle would be torn in this diagram?
Which of the following articulations is unable to assist in elbow function?
rationale: The radiocarpal joint does not have an influence with the elbow functioning.

stem: What structure(s) reinforce the elbow capsule?
rationale: The capsule of the elbow, both anteriorly and posteriorly, is relatively thin and is covered by the brachialis muscle in front and the triceps brachii behind. The capsule is reinforced by the ulnar and radial collateral ligaments.

stem: The bicipital bursa cushions the bicep tendon when the forearm is
rationale: The bicipital bursa lies in the anterior aspect of the bicipital tuberosity and cushions the biceps tendon when the forearm is pronated.

stem: In which of the following movements is the highlighted structure in the given figure of major importance?
rationale: The ulnar collateral ligament is of major importance in activities that produce forceful flexion- supination movements. This ligament plays a key role in maintaining elbow stability against valgus torque.

stem: In the history section of an elbow assessment, what would be considered an inappropriate question to ask the patient?
choices: Is the pain contributed to a sudden injury or overuse?
Is there any movement that makes the pain better or worse?
Do you have any cuts on your arm?
Is there a feeling of locking or catching?
rationale: Observation of the integrity of the skin and other signs is more appropriate for the observation section of the assessment.

stem: During the assessment of an elbow, which of the following would be considered unobservable?
choices: Is an isosceles triangle formed posteriorly?
Has the carrying angle of the elbow joint abnormally increased?
What is the range of motion of the elbow?
Has the carrying angle of the elbow joint abnormally decreased?
Is the patient double-jointed? Is the length of the patient’s arms from the elbows to the fingers uneven?
rationale: The length of both the arms is immaterial to elbow assessment. The carrying angle, the range of motion, and the formation of an isosceles triangle when the elbow is bent to a 45-degree angle and observed from the rear are all points of observation during the assessment of an elbow.

stem: Which of the following body structures is a medial soft tissue that should be palpated in order to assess an elbow injury?
rationale: The ulnar nerve is a medial soft tissue that should be palpated in order to assess an elbow injury. The radial collateral and annular ligaments are laterally placed soft tissues. Triceps are posteriorly located soft tissues, and the pronator teres is an anteriorly placed soft tissue.

stem: The elbow-extension test requires patients to:
choices: flex their shoulders to 90 degrees and then fully extend and lock both elbows.
extend an elbow and pinch it with the fingers of the other hand.
extend their arms while an athletic trainer resists forearm pronation.
extend their arms while an athletic trainer rotates their forearm.
flex their shoulders and then pinch their thumbs and index fingers together.
rationale: The elbow-extension test requires patients to flex their shoulders to 90 degrees and then fully extend and lock both elbows. The injured and uninjured sides are compared and those with equal extension are recorded as full extension. In a positive test, the patient is unable to fully straighten the elbow.

stem: In the following video, what structure of the elbow is being assessed?
rationale: A varus stress test checks for sprain or instability of the lateral, or radial collateral ligament.

stem: In the following video, what structure of the elbow is being assessed?
rationale: Tinel’s sign is designed to determine ulnar nerve compromise.

stem: Which of the following conditions would be incorrect if a patient complained of pain along the medial aspect of the elbow?
rationale: Tennis elbow is associated with pain along the lateral aspect of the elbow.

stem: A middle school tennis player comes to you complaining of sudden pain and locking in the elbow joint. They also have swelling, pain, and crepitation. What could be wrong with this athlete?
rationale: All of these are signs and symptoms of elbow osteochondritis dissecans.

stem: Which of the following is another name for elbow osteochondritis dissecans?
rationale: Elbow osteochondritis dissecans is referred to as Panner’s disease in children under age 10 years.

stem: Which of the following incorrectly describes Little League elbow?
choices: An accelerated apophyseal growth region plus a delay in the medial epicondylar growth plate.
An avulsion of the lateral epicondyle
A nonunion stress fracture of the olecranon epiphysis
A traction apophysitis with a possible fragmentation of the medial epicondylar apophysis
rationale: Little League elbow is described by an avulsion of the medial epicondyle.

stem: Which of the following is incorrectly listed as a factor that can lead to cubital tunnel syndrome?
choices: traction injury from a valgus force
subluxation of the ulnar nerve because of a lax ligament
progressive compression of the ligament on the nerve
rationale: A fracture of the olecranon is not a factor that can lead to cubital tunnel syndrome.

stem: Which of the following statements is true of ulnar collateral ligament injury?
choices: It mostly occurs during actions such as a forehand stroke in tennis.
It is mostly accompanied by severe swelling and can even lead to malignant formations.
It invariably leads to the rupture of the ligament.
It is usually managed by the application of a cold compress for one hour.
It is invariably accompanied by point tenderness in the myotendinous junction.
rationale: The ulnar collateral ligament is mostly injured as a result of repetitive valgus loading on the elbow. This occurs during actions such as a forehand stroke in tennis.

stem: Which of the following statements is true of olecranon bursitis?
choices: It can be managed by applying a cold compress for at least 15 minutes.
It is relieved by a program of conservative treatment primarily involving compression.
It invariably leads to serious health consequences such as the development of malignant tumors.
Cryotherapy is mostly unsuccessful in relieving or managing chronic olecranon bursitis.
It is invariably accompanied by extreme pain and heat.
rationale: Olecranon bursitis occurs when the olecranon bursa of the elbow is inflamed and produces severe swelling, pain, and tenderness. A program of conservative treatment primarily involving compression relieves chronic olecranon bursitis. Follow-up management of olecranon bursitis may include cryotherapy, ultrasound, and rehabilitative exercises.

stem: In the following image of the elbow, which of the following injuries has been sustained?
rationale: The figure demonstrates a posterior elbow dislocation.

stem: If a patient suffers an elbow dislocation, what nerve(s) can be compromised?
rationale: Injury to the median and ulnar nerve often occurs in an elbow dislocation.

stem: A softball catcher complains of loss of motor function of her fingers and sensory loss in her forearm. Her fingers are also beginning to form a contracture. She had an elbow dislocation 3 weeks ago. What is probably wrong with this athlete?
choices: rupture of the extensor muscles of the wrist
rationale: The signs and symptoms are consistent with a Volkmann’s contracture.

stem: What condition is illustrated in the following image of the elbow?
rationale: The inflamed bursa produces pain, severe swelling, and point tenderness and will sometimes appear spontaneously and without usual pain and heat.

stem: Please check all of the injuries that can be caused by falling on an outstretch hand.
Please check all the characteristics of this exercise used to regain elbow extension following an injury.
choices: It is of relatively long duration.
rationale: The exercise must avoid forcing the joint, so it is done slowly with low force to fully extend the elbow and maintain that position.

stem: The humeroradial inferior glide is utilized to increase which of the following?
rationale: Humeroradial inferior glides are used to increase flexion and extension of the elbow and increase the joint space.

stem: What is the primary muscle of the elbow that is being concentrically strengthened in this video?
rationale: The video demonstrates the biceps brachii being strengthened by performing a barbell curl.

stem: In the context of the rehabilitation of the elbow, the initial stage of strength exercises should include
rationale: In the context of the rehabilitation of the elbow, the initial stage of strengthening exercises should include subpainful submaximal isometric exercises. After the elbow has regained some range of motion, the first consideration should be restoration of normal range of movement.

stem: Which of the following would be an inappropriate body structure to palpate during the palpation section of the elbow?
rationale: The joint capsule itself is not palpable, however, the surrounding ligaments are.

stem: What artery does the subclavian become?
rationale: The subclavian artery becomes an axillary artery and then the brachial artery whose branches supply the elbow.

stem: Please check all of the muscles that are innervated by the radial nerve.
Please check all of the statements that are TRUE regarding the one-arm hop test.
choices: There is a 3-minute break between the testing for each upper extremity.
From a push-up position you hop with 1 arm onto a 10.2 cm step and back on to the floor.
You check both the dominant and non-dominant side.
You time how long it takes you to perfom the up-downs.
The one-arm hop test may be useful as a basis for comparing performance of an injured contralateral upper extremity.
From a standing position you drop down and do a push up.

stem: Please check all of the types of sports where medial epicondylitis is common:
Please select the factors that most commonly lead to cubital tunnel syndrome.
choices: traction injury from a valgus torque
subluxation of the radial nerve because of a lax ligament
traction injury from a varus force
subluxation of the ulnar nerve because of a lax ligament
progressive compression of the ligament on the nerve

stem: What artery is involved in a Volkmann’s contracture?
rationale: Volkmann’s contracture is a type of forearm ischemic contracture resulting from a brachial artery injury.

stem: The pinch grip test is checks for
rationale: An inability to touch the thumb and index finger indicates entrapment of the anterior interosseous nerve between the two heads of the pronator muscle.

stem: What condition is noted in this picture?
rationale: A Volkmann’s contracture has the wrist flexed and involvement of the muscles in the forearm that flex and extend the fingers.

stem: Cozen test can be utilized to check for what problem?
Which of the following statements is true of the middle radioulnar joint?
choices: It is an enarthrodial joint that is covered by a thin, fibrous capsule.
It is a pivot joint that moves in a ring formed by the ulna and the annular ligament.
It is held together by an oblique ligamentous cord and the interosseous membrane.
It is a pivot joint formed by the articulation of the head of the ulna with a small notch on the radius.
It is held securely by the anterior and posterior radioulnar ligaments.
rationale: The middle radioulnar joint is held together by an oblique ligamentous cord and the interosseous membrane. The oblique cord is a small band of ligamentous fibers that are attached to the lateral side of the ulna and pass downward and laterally to the radius.

stem: Which of the following cords is a small band of ligamentous fibers that are attached to the lateral side of the ulna and pass downward and laterally to the radius?
rationale: The oblique cord is a small band of ligamentous fibers that are attached to the lateral side of the ulna and pass downward and laterally to the radius. The middle radioulnar joint, which is the junction between the shafts of the ulna and the radius, is held together by an oblique ligamentous cord and the interosseous membrane.

stem: Which of the following nerves controls the majority of extensor muscles?
rationale: The majority of the extensor muscles are controlled by the radial nerve. Except for the flexor carpi ulnaris and half of the flexor digitorum profundus, most of the flexor muscles of the forearm are supplied by the median nerve.

stem: Which of the following nerves enters the palm of the hand through the carpal tunnel and supplies three of the four thenar muscles?
rationale: The median nerve enters the palm of the hand through the carpal tunnel. The median nerve supplies three of the four thenar muscles, and the radial two lumbricals and the ulnar nerve supply the rest of the instrinsic muscles of the hand.

stem: Which of the following nerves supplies most of the flexor muscles of the forearm with the exception of the flexor carpi ulnaris and half of the flexor digitorum profundus?
rationale: The median nerve supplies most of the flexor muscles of the forearm with the exception of the flexor carpi ulnaris and half of the flexor digitorum profundus. The major blood supply to the forearm stems from the brachial artery.

stem: Which of the following statements is true of the superior radioulnar joint?
choices: It is a false joint that is enclosed in a thin, fibrous capsule.
It is held securely by the posterior and anterior radioulnar ligaments.
It is a gliding articulation that is separated by a fibrocartilaginous disk.
It is a pivot joint that moves in a ring formed by the ulna and the annular ligament.
It is held together by an oblique ligamentous cord and the interosseous membrane.
rationale: The forearm has three articulations: the superior, middle, and distal radioulnar joints.

stem: What joint classification is the first carpometacarpal joint?
rationale: The thumb varies slightly at its carpometacarpal joint and is classified as a saddle joint that allows rotation on its long axis in addition to the other metacarpophalangeal movements.

stem: Which of the following is a thenar muscle that pulls the thumb in front of the palm to meet the little finger?
rationale: The opponens pollicis is a thenar muscle that pulls the thumb in front of the palm to meet the little finger. Three thenar muscles act on the thumb.

stem: How many articulations does the forearm have?
choices: one, the middle radioulnar joint between the shafts of the ulna and radius
two, the middle radioulnar joint and distal radioulnar joint.
three, the middle radioulnar joint, distal and superior radioulnar joint
four, the middle radioulnar joint, distal and superior radioulnar joint, and humeroulnar joint
five, the middle radioulnar joint, distal and superior radioulnar joint, humeroulnar joint, and humeroradial joint
rationale: The forearm has three articulations: the superior, middle, and distal radioulnar joints.

stem: Which of the following is a test for carpal tunnel syndrome?
rationale: Phalen's test is used to diagnose carpal tunnel syndrome. In Phalen’s test, the patient is instructed to flex both wrists as far as possible and press them together. This position is held for approximately 1 minute.

stem: Which of the following tests is used to determine the function of the radial and ulnar arteries supplying the hand?
rationale: Allen’s test is used to determine the function of the radial and ulnar arteries supplying the hand. The patient is instructed to squeeze the hand tightly into a fist and then open it fully three or four times. While the patient is holding the last fist, the athletic trainer places firm pressure over each artery. The patient is then instructed to open the hand. The palm should now be blanched. One of the arteries is then released; if normal, the hand will instantly become red. The same process is repeated with the other artery. The hand is next evaluated for sensation alterations, especially in cases of suspected tunnel impingements. Nerve involvements will be further evaluated when active and resistive movements are initiated.

stem: What other injury can the forearm sustain that is much less common than in the lower leg?
rationale: The forearm can also sustain an acute or chronic exertional compartment syndrome, although this condition is much less common than in the lower leg.

stem: Which of the following deformities is caused by a palsy of the radial nerve?
rationale: A palsy of the radial nerve produces a drop wrist deformity, which is caused by paralysis of the extensor muscles such that the wrist and fingers cannot be extended. Palsy of the median nerve can cause an ape hand, in which the thumb is pulled backward in line with the other fingers by the extensor muscles.

stem: Which of the following conditions will occur when a person consistently performs wrist flexion activities?
choices: tendinitis of the flexor digitorum profundus
tendinitis of the abductor pollicis brevis
tendinitis of the flexor carpi ulnaris
tendinitis of the flexor figitorum superficialis
rationale: A person who performs repetitive wrist flexion activities will most likely suffer from tendinitis of the flexor carpi ulnaris or the flexor carpi radialis.

stem: If a patient is suffering from the problem noted in the figure of the hand, which nerve would be damaged?
rationale: Palsy of the median nerve can result in ape hand.

stem: What etiology is accurate to the cause of carpal tunnel syndrome?
rationale: Repetitive wrist flexion can cause carpal tunnel syndrome.

stem: Hoffman’s disease is another name for which of the following?
rationale: Hoffman’s disease is another name for deQuervain’s disease.

stem: What carpal bone is the most likely to dislocate?
rationale: A dislocation of the lunate is considered the most common dislocation of a carpal bone.

stem: What is the most commonly fractured carpal bone?
rationale: A fracture of the scaphoid is considered the most common fracture of a carpal bone.

stem: What motion would cause pain and be indicative of a scaphoid fracture?
choices: Upward pressure on the thumb and radial flexion
Downward pressure on the thumb and radial flexion
rationale: Upward pressure exerted on the thumb and radial flexion are indicative of a scaphoid fracture.

stem: Mallet finger is an injury to which of the following structures?
rationale: Mallet finger is an avulsion of the extensor tendon from its insertion along with a piece of bone.

stem: If a person presents with a boutonniere deformity, what was the mechanism of injury?
choices: DIP is forced into extension and the PIP is forced into flexion
DIP and PIP are forced into extension
DIP and PIP are forced into flexion
DIP is forced into flexion and the PIP is forced into extension
DIP has no injury and the PIP is forced into flexion
rationale: Boutonniere deformity occurs when the DIP is forced into extension and the PIP is forced into flexion.

stem: The figure of the wrist, hand, and fingers demonstrates which of the following conditions?
rationale: Dupuytren’s contracture is a flexor deformity of the ring finger and its cause is unknown.

stem: If a patient complains of pain and swelling over the 5th metacarpal, what type of fracture may have occurred?
rationale: A Boxer’s fracture occurs in the fifth metacarpal.

stem: The diagram represents a taping technique for the wrist, hand, and fingers that could be used for which of the following problems?
rationale: A wrist sprain can benefit by taping the injury.

stem: If a patient is suffering from the problem noted in the figure of the hand, which nerves would be damaged?
rationale: Claw hand results from compression of the median and ulnar nerves.

stem: A tennis player has severe pain near the base of her fourth and fifth metacarpals after accidentally hitting her racket on the court after diving for a ball. What could possibly be the problem?
rationale: Based on the location of the pain and the mechanism of injury, it is likely that they have suffered a fracture of the hamate.

stem: Select the symptoms that are related to the injury of the forearm, wrist, hand, and fingers. Forearm Contusion
Select the symptoms that are related to the injury of the forearm, wrist, hand, and fingers. Forearm Splints
Select the symptoms that are related to the injury of the forearm, wrist, hand, and fingers. Forearm Fractures
Select the symptoms that are related to the injury of the forearm, wrist, hand, and fingers. Colles’ Fracture
If a patient complains of pain and swelling over the thumb, what type of fracture may have occurred?
rationale: A Bennett’s fracture occurs in the first metacarpal just distal to the carpometacarpal joint of the thumb as the result of an axial and abduction force to the thumb.

stem: The video demonstrates radiocarpal joint anterior glides. These are used to increase what motion?
rationale: Radiocarpal joint anterior glides increase wrist extension.

stem: The video demonstrates exercises that should be used to accomplish which of the following?
rationale: Regaining grip strength is done by the activities demonstrated in the video.

stem: What group of muscles is being stretched in this video?
rationale: Wrist extensors are being stretched in this video.

stem: The video demonstrates an exercise that would be useful for a person suffering from which of the following problems?
rationale: Wrist rolls will strengthen both the flexors and extensors of the wrist.

stem: The video demonstrates concentric and eccentric strengthening for which of the following muscles?
rationale: Radial deviation is being demonstrated and can be caused by the flexor carpi radialis.

stem: Which of the following would be an inappropriate body structure to palpate during the palpation section of the forearm?
rationale: The lumbricals are intrinsic muscles located in the hand.

stem: Where is the triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) located in the wrist and hand?
choices: It is interposed between the head of the ulna and the triquetral bone.
It is the opening of the carpal tunnel that allows structures to pass through to the hand.
It is interposed between the trapezoid and the scaphoid bones of the carpal region.
It is the tunnel under the extensor retinaculum that allows structures to pass through to the hand.
It is interposed between the5th Fifth metacarpal phalangeal base and the pisiform bone.
rationale: The concave surfaces of the lower ends of the radius and the ulna articulate with the convex surfaces of the first row of carpal bones, with the exception of the pisiform, which articulates with the TFCC interposed between the head of the ulna and the triquetral bone.

stem: The radiocarpal joint is a condyloid joint. What does this mean?
choices: A condyloid joint is an articulation between two irregular-shaped bones.
A condyloid joint is an articulation that allows movement around two axis.
A condyloid joint is an articulation that allows gliding.
A condyloid joint is an articulation that allows movements except axial rotation.
A condyloid joint is an articulation that is connected by fibrous tissue.
rationale: The radiocarpal joint is a condyloid joint and permits flexion, extension, abduction, and circumduction.

stem: What joint classification is the first carpometacarpal joint?
rationale: The thumb varies slightly at its carpometacarpal joint and is classified as a saddle joint that allows rotation on its long axis in addition to the other metacarpophalangeal movements.

stem: What nerve can become compressed in the carpal tunnel?
rationale: The transverse carpal ligament serves as the roof of the carpal tunnel, in which the median nerve is often compressed.

stem: Please check all of the mechanisms of injury that can cause a triangular fibrocartilage complex (TFCC) injury.
choices: violent twist or torque of the wrist
crushing of the wrist by a helmet, ball, etc

stem: What will be a sign or symptom of tenosynovitis as compared to tendinitis?
choices: isometric exercises produce pain and weakness
tenderness and pain over the tendon
rationale: A major complaint of a person who is suffering from tenosynovitis is tenderness and pain over the tendon. This will not be a sign or symptom that a person suffering from tendinitis will have.

stem: If a patient is suffering from Bishop’s hand, what nerve has been damaged?
rationale: Damage to the ulnar nerve can result in Bishop's or Benediction deformity.

stem: What problem or condition is noted in this figure of the wrist, hand, and finger?
rationale: The figure demonstrated a wrist ganglion.

stem: A rupture of the flexor digitorum profundus is also referred to as which of the following?
rationale: A rupture of the flexor digitorum profundus is often referred to as Jersey finger.

stem: Select only the appropriate steps that may be taken when releasing blood from beneath a fingernail.
choices: First, coat the injured finger with antifungal cream once the hole is created.
Use a high-temperature cautery while using moderate pressure to melt through the nail to the site of the bleeding.
Use a needle that has been sanitized by a burning flame.
First, coat the injured finger with antibiotic cream once the hole is created.
Use a small gauge drill to penetrate the injured nail, using a rotary action.

stem: What rehabilitation technique is the patient doing in the two figures to address the median and radial nerve?
rationale: If a patient is experiencing neural signs, such as tingling, numbness, or pain, stretching to relieve neural tension in the median and radial nerves reduces those symptoms.

stem: What is the only clinical prediction rule used for the hand?
rationale: Carpal tunnel syndrome is the one established clinical prediction rule that identifies patients who likely have CTS based on specific patient characteristics.

stem: The insertion of the palmaris longus is at the
What muscle flexes the wrist and the phalanges?
What is the special test to check for deQuervain’s syndrome?
What anatomical structure is located in this area?
What characteristic is wrong about the atlas vertebra?
choices: It sits below the axis vertebra.
It has no body or spinous process.
Its lateral masses are connected to the anterior and posterior arches.
Its arches create a large enough opening to allow the medulla of the spinal cord to pass through.
Its upper surfaces articulate with the occipital condyles.
rationale: The atlas, named for its function of supporting the head, is the first cervical vertebra and sits on the axis, or C2.

stem: What section of the spine is known as false vertebrae?
rationale: The spine, or vertebral columns, is composed of 39 individual bones, in which 24 are movable, or true, and nine are classified as immovable, or false.

stem: The sacroiliac is the junction formed by the sacrum and the __________.
rationale: The sacrum articulates with the ilium to form the sacroiliac joint.

stem: What is the correct numerical order for the three different divisions of the movable vertebrae, respectively – cervical, thoracic, lumbar?
rationale: The first division comprises the seven cervical vertebrae; the second, the twelve thoracic vertebrae; and the third, the five lumbar vertebrae.

stem: What structure formed by the ilia anteriorly meeting and has an interarticular disk with a synovium and is lubricated by synovial fluid?
rationale: The ilia meet anteriorly to form the pubis symphysis which has an interarticular disk with a synovium and is lubricated by synovial fluid. The sacrum articulates with the ilia to form two sacroiliac joints posteriorly.

stem: What statement is true of the movements of the lumbar region of the vertebral column?
choices: It restricts flexion but allows ample rotation.
It allows ample rotation but restricts extension.
rationale: The lumbar region of the vertebral column allows limited rotation. It also allows flexion, extension, and limited lateral flexion.

stem: A major ligament that joins the various vertebral structures is the __________.
rationale: A major ligament that joins the various vertebral structures is the interspinous ligament. Other such ligaments are the anterior longitudinal, the posterior longitudinal, the supraspinous, and the ligamentum flavum, which are important stablizers of the spine.

stem: What structure contains the longissimus column of muscles?
rationale: The erector spinae are a group of paired muscles that consist of three columns, or bands. These bands are the iliocostalis, the longissimus, and the spinalis.

stem: What percentage value of flexion occurs at the lumbosacral junction (L5-S1)?
rationale: Seventy-five percent of flexion occurs at the lumbosacral junction (L5-S1), whereas 15 percent to 20 percent occurs between L4 and L5.

stem: How many pairs of the spinal nerves extend from the sides of the spinal cord?
rationale: Thirty-one pairs of the spinal nerves consist of eight cervical, twelve thoracic, five lumbar, five sacral, and one coccygeal.

stem: What statement about spinal nerves and the peripheral branches is wrong?
choices: The sacral plexus is comprised of L4 – S4.
A dermatome is the area of cutaneous sensory distribution.
A myotome is a muscle innvervated by a single specific spinal nerve.
The brachial plexus is comprised of C7 – T1.
A plexus is a network of nerves.
rationale: The brachial plexus is comprised of spinal nerve roots C5 – T1.

stem: In the observation section of the assessment of the spine, what is considered an appropriate action to perform?
choices: The entire body should be observed only from the lateral angle.
Special attention should be paid to the low back, pelvis, and hips.
Use of a posture screen should be avoided.
Special devices should be used even by trained observers with good background in postural observation.
All postural asymmetries should be considered.
rationale: In the observation section of the assessment of the spine, special attention should be paid to the low back, pelvis, and hips. The entire body should be observed from all angles. To ensure accuracy of observation, a plumb line or posture screen may be used.

stem: What would be an INAPPROPRIATE body structure to palpate during the palpation section of the cervical spine?
choices: space between the lumbar spinous processes
rationale: Palpation the anterior superior iliac spines (ASIS) is not necessary for the cervical spine, but it is used as an alignment landmark when performing a postural assessment of the anterior body.

stem: Bakody's sign is associated with what special test?
rationale: A positive shoulder abduction test used to assess the cervical spine is known as Bakody's sign. In this test, the patient abducts the shoulder by placing the hand on top of the head.

stem: In the video, what would be indicative if the special test for the cervical spine resulted in a positive finding?
choices: cervical vertebral artery is being partially occluded because of some abnormal compression
nerve root compression or herniated disk
rationale: When performing the vertebral artery test, if the patient becomes dizzy or experiences nystagmus it is considered a positive finding and should be referred to a physician.

stem: In the video, what is the purpose of the special test for the lumbar spine?
choices: to observe symmetry of lumbar movement
to increase the pain (to painful side) or indicate presence of a herniated disk
to reproduce pain with possible indication of spondylolysis
to observe synced motion of the PSISs on each side
to detect an increase in nerve root tension, or neural tension
rationale: When performing a lumbar lateral rotation, the pelvis should not move during the rotation and there should be symmetry in the movement of the lumbar spine.

stem: If the special test of the spine demonstrated in the video is positive, what could possibly be wrong with the patient?
rationale: The reverse straight leg raise test can be performed to check for a L4 nerve root irritation.

stem: What is the name of the special test of the spine being performed in the video?
rationale: The spring test is when a downward pressure is applied through the spinous process of each vertabra to assess anterior/posterior motion.

stem: What special test associate with the spine is demonstrated in the figure?
rationale: The figure demonstrates sacral compression, which is indicative of a sacral joint irritation or sprain.

stem: Select the structure(s) of the spine that is/are being assessed in the lumbar spine special test video.
Select the structure(s) of the spine that is/are being assessed in the lumbar spine special test video.

What are the anatomical malalignments of the spine and other involved structures in this figure?
choices: kyphotic position that causes compensatory position of the head and neck
an abnormal curvature in one direction and a compensatory secondary curve in the opposite direction
increase in both the anterior tilt of the pelvis and in hip flexion
posterior shift of the thoracic segment and anterior shifting of the entire pelvis
increased thoracic curve and protracted scapulae producing a rounded shoulder appearance
rationale: The image illustrates a swayback posture with a decrease in lordosis in the lumbar spine and an increase in kyphosis in the thoracic spine.

stem: In an occupational educational program for the prevention of spine injuries, what would be one of the most important factors to address?
rationale: Addressing and being conscience of proper body mechanics will prove to be a significant reducer of occupational injuries to the spine.

stem: In the context of cervical disk injuries, what action is most likely to facilitate a stable, safe, and pain-free cervical posture during strenuous activity?
choices: asking the patient to perform isotonic exercises
asking the patient to perform range of motion (ROM) exercises
asking the patient to perform isometric exercises
instructing the patient on the importance of maintaining a neutral spine
rationale: Cervical disk injuries can be managed by instructing the patient on the importance of maintaining a neutral spine. Initial treatment involves rest and immobilization of the neck to decrease discomfort.

stem: What would differentiate a cervical dislocation from a cervical fracture?
choices: a cervical fracture would have a significant amount of pain, while the cervical dislocation would not.
a cervical dislocation would have a significant amount of pain, while the cervical fracture would not.
a cervical dislocation would involve the neck to be tilted toward the dislocated side with extreme muscle tightness, while the cervical fracture would not.
a cervical fracture would involve the neck to be tilted toward the dislocated side with extreme muscle tightness, while the cervical dislocation would not.
numbness would occur in a cervical fracture, whereas it would not occur in a cervical dislocation.
rationale: The most easily discernible difference between a cervical dislocation and a cervical fracture is that in a cervical dislocation, the neck would be tilted toward the dislocated side along with extreme muscle tightness.

stem: A football player complains of tenderness to the transverse and spinous processes of the neck that appeared the day following a game. What could be wrong with this player?
rationale: A cervical sprain may not display pain initially but always appears the day after trauma. Tenderness will be over the transverse and spinous process.

stem: What is caused by an injury to one side of the spinal cord that results in loss of motor function, touch, vibration, and position sense on one side of the body?
rationale: A brown-sequard lesion is caused by an injury to one side of the spinal cord that results in loss of motor function, touch, vibration, and position sense on one side of the body.

stem: What is caused by hemorrhage or ischemia in the central portion of the cord and results in complete quadriplegia with nonspecific sensory loss in sexual as well as bowel-bladder dysfunction?
rationale: Central cord syndrome is caused by hemorrhage or ischemia in the central portion of the cord and results in complete quadriplegia with nonspecific sensory loss in sexual as well as bowel- bladder dysfunction.

stem: Brachial plexus neuropraxia occurs when the neck is __________.
choices: forced laterally to the opposite side while the shoulder is depressed
flexed while the shoulder is depressed
extended while the shoulder is depressed
forced laterally to the opposite side while the shoulder is elevated
flexed while the shoulder is elevated
rationale: The mechanism of injury for a brachial plexus neurapraxia is forced laterally to the opposite side while the shoulder is depressed.

stem: If at the end of a physically active day a young patient complains of a backache and tight hamstrings and he has a noticeable kyphosis of the thoracic spine and lumbar lordosis, what is the suspect condition?
rationale: The signs and symptoms of Scheuermann’s disease are noticeable kyphosis of the thoracic spine, lumbar lordosis, and back pain at the end of a physical day.

stem: Hyperflexion of the trunk or falling from a height and landing on the feet or buttocks could cause what?
rationale: Hyperflexion of the trunk or falling from a height and landing on your feet or buttocks could cause a compression fracture.

stem: Pain in the low back that is created in active extension and passive flexion is indicative of what?
rationale: Pain in the low back that is created in active extension and passive flexion is indicative of a low back muscle strain.

stem: When addressing myofascial pain syndrome, the piriformis is an important muscle to address because of its close proximity to what nerve?
rationale: The sciatic nerve either pierces or runs above or below the piriformis. A trigger point could negatively affect the sciatic nerve.

stem: Spondylolisthesis most likely occurs at what vertebral segment?
rationale: [Re-keyed to current sports-medicine consensus: isthmic spondylolisthesis (the athletic type, from an L5 pars defect) most commonly occurs at L5-S1. (Degenerative spondylolisthesis is most common at L4-L5.)] Spondylolisthesis most likely occurs at L4-L5 and is often referred to as a step deformity.

stem: Spondylolisthesis is considered to be a complication of what?
rationale: Spondylolisthesis is considered to be a complication of spondylolysis.

stem: If a person has pain that increases after activity and is hesitant to perform forward bending, what could be wrong?
choices: spondylolysis, spondylolisthesis, or a herniated disc
rationale: A person suffering from spondylolysis or spondylolisthesis may have pain that increases after activity and often they are hesitant to perform forward bending.

stem: A track athlete suffers from pain running up and down hills and notices that the pain radiates into the anterior thigh. Based on the signs and symptoms, he is most likely suffering from what?
rationale: The athlete will have pain running up and down hills and pain often radiates into their anterior thigh with a sacrioiliac sprain.

stem: What statement is true of back pain?
choices: Adolescents have a 70%–80% incidence of back pain before the age of 20 years.
People aged between 20 and 30 years are more prone to developing chronic low back pain than others.
Males are more likely to suffer from low back pain than females.
Recurrent low back pain is caused exclusively by nerve root compression.
Recurrent low back pain is rare, following an episode of low back pain.
rationale: Adolescents have a 70%–80% incidence of back pain before the age of 20 years.Following an episode of low back pain, it is likely that a patient will have further recurrent episodes of pain.Females are more likely to suffer from low back pain than males.

stem: An athlete who has previously suffered from brachial plexus neuropraxia and wishes to return to full activity must definitely have a negative __________.
rationale: An athlete who has previously suffered from brachial plexus neuropraxia and wishes to return to full activity must have a negative Spurling's test. The athlete should also have full cervical and shoulder range of motion (ROM) and the strength to be able to return to play, since there can be associated shoulder weakness due to the nerve irritation.

stem: In the video, what back muscle is being addressed with the myofascial release technique?
rationale: The video demonstrate the quadratus lumborum being released, which originates on the twelfth rib and the transverse processes of L1 through L4.

stem: The back stretch demonstrated in the video would be most effective for a person who is suffering from what?
rationale: Stretching the piriformis can assist in relieving pain caused by sciatica.

stem: If a patient complains of morning pain when doing the activity in the video, what could be wrong?
rationale: A herniated disc will cause pain when side bending and the patient's pain will be worse in the morning.

stem: The techniques demonstrated in this video can be used when a patient suffers from what spine injury?
rationale: Cervical side bending mobilizations treat pain and stiffness where there is some resistance to side bending.

stem: What muscle of the spine is being stretched in this video?
rationale: The sternocleidomastoid is being stretched in this video.

stem: What is the name of the lateral neck stretching technique demonstrated in the video?
rationale: The video demonstrates the lateral neck flexors being stretched.

stem: In the context of the treatment for a small protrusion of the nucleus pulposus, how often is traction usually applied to a patient?
choices: once a day for a week
twice a day for a week
daily, five times per week, for two weeks
twice a day for two weeks
three times a day until pain is relieved
rationale: Traction is usually applied daily (five times per week) for two weeks.

stem: What is the main goal of the joint mobilization for the spine that is performed in this video?
choices: to increase space between transverse processes
to decrease space of the intervetebral foramen
to reduce pain and guarding around the sacroiliac joint
to increase mobility of the individual vertebrae
rationale: Lumbar lateral distractions are done in order to reduce pain associated with some compression of a spinal nerve. This can be done by increasing the space between the transverse processes.

stem: Select the muscle(s) that is being stretched in the video.
choices: the neural tissues of the upper spine

stem: Select the structure(s) that is being stretched in the video.
choices: the neural tissues of the upper spine

stem: What factors should be considered in a rehabilitation program for a patient suffering from low back pain?
choices: any previous thoracic injury that may have been sustained
any previous cervical injury that may have been sustained
any previous sacral injury that may have been sustained
rationale: The workplace environment of patients suffering from low back pain is to be considered in a rehabilitation program. Other factors that are to be considered in rehabilitation are any previous lumbar injury that the patient may have sustained, psychosocial issues, fitness level, and training goals.

stem: An abdominal brace or "drawing in" maneuver similar to a posterior tilt of the pelvis, which flattens the lumbar curve, is caused by a simultaneous co-contraction of the transversus abdominus and __________ muscles.
rationale: An abdominal brace or "drawing in" maneuver similar to a posterior tilt of the pelvis, which flattens the lumbar curve, is caused by a simultaneous co-contraction of the transversus abdominus and multifidus muscles. This is done to maintain a "corset" control of the lumbar spine.

stem: A person is suffering from a lumbosacral strain and needs to perform various rehabilitation exercises. Please identify which phase of healing is appropriate for the rehabilitative exercise indicated.
choices: the acute injury tissue healing phase
