stem: Please check all of the muscles that are innervated by the femoral nerve.
In the observation section of a spine assessment, what would be considered a wrong action to perform?
choices: Does the patient have PEARL pupils?
Is the patient willing to move the head and neck freely?
Does the midline of the posterior spine create a symmetrical division?
Is one shoulder lower than the other?
rationale: Assessing papillary response to light is part of a special test section of a head and/or neck injury assessment.

stem: What should be ruled out before performing any special tests of the cervical spine?
choices: prior injury to the injured body part
prior injury to a related body part
direct trauma to either the vertebrae or spinal cord
radicular pain into the distal extremities
rationale: Special tests for the cervical spine should not be done until trauma to either the vertebrae or the spinal cord has been ruled out.

stem: At what level of the vertebral column is a cervical fracture most likely to occur?
rationale: Cervical factures are most common in C4, C5, or C6.

stem: For what pathology would the FADDIR test be used?
What is the most important consideration in reducing injuries to the head and surrounding area?
choices: standardizing the design and quality of all helmets
putting warning labels on all protective equipment for this area of the body
eliminating protective headgear to reduce the athlete’s want to make contact with this part of the body
educating in proper techniques and the dangers associated with brain injuries
limiting contact sports to post-adolescent sporting activities
rationale: The single most important consideration in reducing injuries to this region involves educating the athlete in 2. proper techniques for initiating contact, 3. the danger of concussive brain injury and the second impact syndrome, and 4. the danger of concussive brain injury and second-impact syndrome.

stem: Please check all the sports that are identified with a high risk for head injuries.
The skull is composed of how many bones?
rationale: The skull is composed of twenty-two bones.

stem: What part of the brain coordinates all voluntary muscle activities and interprets sensory impulses in addition to controlling higher mental functions?
rationale: The cerebrum, also known as the cortex, coordinates all voluntary muscle activities and interprets sensory impulses in addition to controlling higher mental functions. It is the largest part of the brain and is divided into two hemispheres separated by a deep longitudinal fissure.

stem: What structure of the meningeal membranes helps contain the spinal fluid?
rationale: The space between the arachnoid and the pia mater, the membrane that helps contain the spinal fluid, is called the subarachnoid space.

stem: What testing should be included in an on-the-field neurological exam for head injury?
rationale: On-the-field neurological exam for head injury consists of six major areas: cerebral testing, which assesses cognitive function; cranial nerve testing; cerebellar testing, which assesses coordination and motor function; sensory testing; reflex testing; and motor testing. Athletic trainers should administer this exam in all cases of head injuries.

stem: If a patient is unconscious after sustaining a head injury, what is the best method of removal from the playing arena?
rationale: A patient who has been unconscious should be removed from the field on a spine board.

stem: In the Balance Error Scoring System (BESS), what is the angle of hip flexion at which the patient must maintain the contralateral limb during the single-leg stance?
rationale: In the Balance Error Scoring System (BESS), patients are asked to maintain the contralateral limb in 20 to 30 degrees of hip flexion and 40 to 50 degrees of knee flexion during the single-leg stance. The patient is also asked to stand quietly and as motionless as possible in the stance position, keeping the hands on the iliac crest and eyes closed.

stem: What question can be asked during the history section of a head assessment to determine antegrade amnesia?
choices: Can you remember walking off the field?
Can you remember the score or who we played last week?
Can you tell me what happened to you?
Can you move your hands and feet?
rationale: Antegrade amnesia refers to a loss of memory that happened after the traumatic event. A question of this nature will determine whether there is antegrade amnesia.

stem: In the observation section of a head assessment, what would be considered an incorrect action to perform
choices: Is the patient able to determine who the opponent is?
Does the patient have delayed motor responses?
Do the patient’s eyes track smoothly?
Does the patient elicit a normal emotional response?
Is the patient’s scalp swollen or bleeding?
rationale: Observing the patient’s eyes tracking ability would be more appropriate to perform during the special tests section of the head assessment.

stem: In the case of a suspected head injury, an on-the-field neurological exam should be performed. Which of the six major areas of the exam is missing from this list? Cerebral testing Motor testing Cranial nerve testing Reflex testing Cerebellar testing
rationale: Sensory testing involves the assessment of the dermatomes.

stem: What is an INCORRECT process to perform for a head assessment when testing the function of the eyes?
rationale: A fluorescent strip is used to detect a corneal abrasion and/or laceration.

stem: What is one of the domains of the Vestibular/Ocular Motor Screening (VOMS) assessment?
choices: width of the field of vision
rationale: The Vestibular/Ocular Motor Screening (VOMS) assessment includes 5 domains: 5. smooth pursuit, 6. horizontal and vertical saccades, 7. near point of convergence (NPC) distance, 8. horizontal vestibular ocular reflex (VOR), and 9. visual motion sensitivity (VMS). This screening tool has been developed to identify concussed athletes by assessing eye movements. A saccade is the quick, simultaneous movement of both eyes between two or more phases of fixation in the same direction.

stem: What is being performed for a head assessment in the following figures?
rationale: The BESS involves three different stances (double, single, and tandem) that are completed twice, once while on a firm surface and once while on a 10-cm thick piece of medium-density foam for a total of six trials.

stem: What is the most serious problem associated with skull fractures?
choices: rupture of the orbital floor due to blood accumulation
leakage of cerebrospinal fluid from the ear, or Battle’s sign
rationale: A possible complication of a skull fracture could be from intracranial bleeding, bone fragments embedded in the brain, and/or infection.

stem: On what account(s) have classification systems for grades of concussions been updated and modified?
choices: Updates and modifications are because of the increase of severity typically associated with sports-related concussions.
Updates and modifications are because loss of consciousness occurs in less than 10 percent of all concussions.
Updates and modifications are because amnesia is found in less than 30 percent of all concussions.
Updates and modifications are because the loss of unconsciousness occurs in less than 10 percent of all concussions.
Updates and modifications are because the Glasgow Coma Scale is outdated and not relevant to the signs and symptoms of a concussion.
rationale: Several of the more recent classification systems account for other parameters (other than just amnesia and unconsciousness) such as concentration deficits, attention span difficulties, and balance and coordination problems rather than focusing on loss of consciousness and posttraumatic amnesia.

stem: After sustaining a cerebral injury, what must the athlete be free of before returning to competition?
rationale: Postconcussive may be present intermittingly, or never present after a cerebral injury is sustained. Even after postconcussive symptoms have disappeared and the patient has returned to play, there is still a danger of recurrent concussion that can produce cumulative traumatic injury to the brain.

stem: What are approved actions for home instructions for a concussion?
rationale: Home instructions for concussion allow for sleep, consumption of acetaminophen for headaches, rest, use an ice pack on head/neck for comfort, and eat a carbohydrate-rich diet.

stem: Since 2009, all 50 states passed some form of concussion legislation that minimally stated what?
choices: The athlete is allowed to return to play on the day of the injury.
The athlete is not allowed to return to play on the day of the injury.
The athlete is allowed to return to play later on the day of the injury, if asymptomatic.
The athlete is allowed to remain in the game if only one concussion symptom is present.
The athlete may return to play/practice with a nurse's medical clearance.
rationale: The athlete is not allowed to return to play on the day of the injury even after becoming symptom-free, in addition to must be immediately removed from even, and can only return to play with written physician clearance.

stem: Once a patient has sustained an initial cerebral concussion, how much greater is the likelihood of her incurring a second one compared to an individual who has never sustained a concussion?
rationale: Once a patient has sustained an initial cerebral concussion, his or her chances of incurring a second one are three to six times greater than for a patient who has never sustained a concussion.

stem: What is a marker for allowing a patient to progress with exercise testing following a concussion?
rationale: In general, the protocol for exercise tolerance allows for individuals to be progressively exerted until the point their symptoms are mildly exacerbated, and the heart rate at that point is used as a marker and can be reassessed over time to evaluate exercise tolerance.

stem: What complication associated with skull fractures requires immediate hospitalization?
choices: rupture of the orbital floor due to blood accumulation
leakage of cerebrospinal fluid from the ear, or Battle’s sign
intracranial bleeding from embedded bone fragments in the brain
rationale: A possible complication of a skull fracture from intracranial bleeding could be bone fragments embedded in the brain, and infection.

stem: What are common signs and symptoms of an epidural hematoma?
choices: weakness on both sides of the body
paralysis on opposite side of brain injury
rationale: In most cases with an epidural hematoma, initially, there is a loss of consciousness, gradually symptoms will worsen such as severe head pains, dizziness, nausea, sleepiness, and dilation of one pupil that is usually on the same side as the injury.

stem: What signs and symptoms may be present with persistent symptoms after a concussion?
rationale: Other may include persistent headache, emotional/sleep/visual disturbances, impaired memory, lack of concentration, sadness and irritability, balance problems, nausea and vomiting, and fatigue. The related symptoms can begin immediately or within several days following the initial trauma and may last for weeks or even months before resolving.

stem: What specific head injury is managed as a life-threatening emergency that must be addressed within approximately five minutes by dramatic lifesaving measures performed in an emergency care facility?
rationale: Second-impact syndrome is a life-threatening emergency that must be addressed within approximately 5 minutes by dramatic lifesaving measures performed in an emergency care facility.

stem: With a scalp injury, what would warrant the need for a physician referral?
choices: if the wound will not quit bleeding after 10 minutes
if the wound has impacted dirt within it
if the wound cannot be located on the scalp
if the wound is more than ½ inch (1.25 cm) in length and 1/8 inch (0.3 cm) in depth
if the wound is not closable with steri-strips or butterfly closures
rationale: Wounds more than 1.25 cm in length and 0.3 cm in depth should be referred to a physician for treatment.

stem: What is a self-reported symptom scale used as a tool for a clinical evaluation of a concussion?
rationale: The Graded Symptom Checklist (GSC) is a self-report symptom scale that the patient completes selecting any of the symptoms and recording the time of when they are present.

stem: What is the purpose of the Sport Concussion Office Assessment Tool (SCOAT6)?
choices: It is designed for patients who work in a factory.
It is designed for patients who work in a corporate setting.
It is to be used 36 hours following the injury.
It is to be used in a quiet, distraction-free setting.
It is to be used with adolescents.
rationale: The Sport Concussion Office Assessment Tool (SCOAT6) should be used following the on-the- field Sport Concussion Assessment Tool (SCAT6) in an off-field or office-based site where it is quiet, distraction-free.

stem: What specific facial injury presents with a locked-open position of the jaw, with jaw movement being almost impossible?
rationale: An overriding malocclusion of the teeth will also be present with a mandibular dislocation.

stem: What structure is most likely to be involved in a "Lefort fracture"?
choices: the lateral wall of an eye socket
the lateral wall of the nasal cavity
rationale: A "Lefort fracture" involves the lateral wall of the nasal cavity. This type of fracture is usually only seen when severe trauma is applied to the maxilla, such as in a motor vehicle accident.

stem: What is a sign of facial laceration?
choices: abrupt increase in the secretion of sweat
swelling of the front of the face
rationale: Patients who have suffered facial lacerations feel pain, and there is substantial bleeding and obvious tearing of the epidermis, the dermis, and often the subcutaneous layer of skin. Patients should be referred to a physician for definitive care, such as suturing.

stem: What structure is most often the source of hemorrhages of the nose?
choices: the cribriform plate of the ethmoid
the horizontal plate of the palatine bone
the posterior portion of the superior concha
the highly vascular anterior aspect of the nasal septum
the termination of the nasolacrimal duct
rationale: Hemorrhages of the nose arise most often from the highly vascular anterior aspect of the nasal septum. In most situations, the nosebleed presents only a minor problem and stops spontaneously after a short period of time.

stem: What is the least amount of time it takes for a septal hematoma to show up after a nasal injury?
rationale: A septal hematoma is a serious complication that occurs secondary to nasal or facial trauma and, although rare will not show up for at least 24–72 hrs after the nasal injury. The two most common symptoms are persistent low-grade pain in the septal area and persistent nasal congestion, usually more on one side than the other.

stem: What statements is correct regarding a tooth fracture?
choices: An uncomplicated crown fracture requires immediate treatment by a dentist.
A complicated crown fracture requires immediate treatment by a dentist.
If a root fracture occurs, an athlete should not continue to play the game.
Fractured teeth always occur with a concussion.
An athlete can continue to play with a type of tooth fracture as long as there are no other signs of a bone fracture or concussion.
rationale: A patient may continue to play but should seek immediate dental attention following a root fracture. If they suffer from a complicated or uncomplicated crown fracture, they can play and should seek dental attention within 24/48 hours.

stem: If a patient has a luxation of the tooth, what does this mean?
choices: The tooth has been knock out of their mouth.
The tooth is not fractured, but is very loose and has moved either forward or backward.
The tooth is fractured and the piece that remains in the mouth is very loose.
rationale: In a tooth luxation, the tooth is not fractured but is very loose and has moved either forward or backward.

stem: What method would be INCORRECT in trying to stop an epistaxis?
choices: a piece of rolled gauze placed between the upper lip and gum
a cold compress placed over the nose
place pressure over the affected nostril
tilting the head slightly back with a cold compress over the nose
place gauze that contains tannic acid in the nostril
rationale: Tilting the head back will cause blood to run down the patient’s throat and could cause nausea.

stem: A LeFort fracture can involve several bones, in particular, the __________.
rationale: A LeFort fracture can involve several bones, in particular, the maxilla.

stem: A patient reports after a plane trip to Hawaii complaining of nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and extreme pain in the ear. What could be a plausible clinical diagnosis?
rationale: Individuals traveling on an airplane and who exhibit nausea, vomiting, dizziness, and extreme pain in their ear often suffer from a ruptured tympanic membrane.

stem: What condition is present in this figure?
rationale: The figure shows auricular hematoma or cauliflower ear.

stem: What sports in collegiate and secondary schools have the highest incidences of eye injuries?
choices: men’s basketball and track and field
rationale: Women’s field hockey and basketball and men’s basketball and wrestling have the highest eye injury rates.

stem: What best describes a hyphema?
choices: collection of blood within the anterior chamber
rationale: A blunt blow to the anterior aspect of the eye can produce a hyphema, which is a collection of blood within the anterior chamber of the eye.

stem: If a patient has swelling of the whole eyelid with itching or burning, what condition may be present?
choices: rupture of the globe of the eye
rationale: The above mentioned are the signs and symptoms of acute conjunctivitis or pink eye.

stem: If a patient cannot breathe and is expiring frothy blood after getting hit in the throat with a baseball, what would most likely be the sustained injury?
choices: a rupture of their vocal chords
rationale: Fractures of the throat cartilage are rare, but it is possible and may be indicated by an inability to breathe and by expiring frothy blood.

stem: What bacteria causes blepharitis?
rationale: Staphylococcal bacteria is often the cause of stye and blepharitis. Blepharitis is a low-grade infection of the eyelid margins near the base of the lashes.

stem: If a patient is able to read line four on the shown figure, what does this result mean?
choices: The patient is able to stand 20 feet away and read what a person with 20/20 vision could read at 50 feet away.
The patient is able to stand 50 feet away and read what a person with 20/20 vision could read at 20 feet away.
The patient is able to see 50% of what a person with 20/20 vision could see.
The patient is able to see 30% of what a person with 20/20 vision could see.
The patient is four times more likely to have vision problems.
rationale: If a person has 20/50 vision, it means that at 20 feet from the chart that person can read letters that a person with 20/20 vision could read from 50 feet away.

stem: Please check what could cause an epistaxis.
choices: a foreign body lodged in the nose

stem: Upon reading a patient’s chart, it is reported the patient is experiencing diplopia. What does this mean?
choices: The patient is seeing flashes of light.
The patient is hearing high-pitched noises.
The patient is having trouble hearing.
rationale: If a patient is suffering from diplopia, they are seeing double.

stem: What is non-related symptom for a maxillary fracture?
rationale: Auras, which involve the visual sensation of fire or glow, are not a symptom associated with a maxillary fracture.

stem: Select all the sports with reported high risks of head injury.
What does the King-Devick test measure?
choices: the patient’s ability to rapidly respond to cognitive questions
the patient’s ability to rapidly react to verbal commands
the patient’s ability to rapidly perform physical agility skills
the patient’s ability to rapidly perform concussion sideline testing
the patient’s ability to rapidly move the eyes while reading
rationale: The King-Devick is a measure of saccadic (quick) eye movement, attention, and processing speed. It is a 2-minute, sideline assessment of rapid number naming that requires one to read a series of variably spaced single-digit numbers on 3 test cards as quickly as possible.

stem: Select all of the types of errors that are marked for a maximum error score of 10 when using the BESS Test.
choices: remaining out of testing position >3 seconds
remaining out of testing position >5 seconds
lifting toe or rocking back on heel

stem: What is malignant brain edema syndrome?
choices: It occurs in the geriatric population from hyperemia.
It occurs in middle-aged adults due to an intracranial clot.
It occurs in the young population due to vascular engorgement with little or no injury to the brain.
It occurs in the pre-pubescent population due to repetitive concussions.
It occurs in babies due to shaken baby syndrome.
rationale: This condition could be resultant due to an intracranial clot, but in children there is diffuse brain swelling resulting from hyperemia or vascular engorgement with little or no injury to the brain.

stem: What factor predisposes the temporomandibular joint to instability?
What is/are the purpose(s) of the external and internal intercostal muscles located in the thoracic and abdominal region of the body?
choices: They provide lubrication to allow the lungs to slide along the thoracic cage with expiration and inspiration of the lungs.
The external intercostal muscles depress the rib cage; the internal intercostal muscles elevate the diaphragm.
The external intercostal muscles elevate the rib cage; the internal intercostal muscles depress the diaphragm.
The external intercostal muscles elevate the diaphragm; the intercostal muscles muscles depress the rib cage.
The external intercostal muscles assist with expiration; the internal intercostal muscles assist with inspiration.
rationale: The external intercostal muscles elevate the diaphragm during inspiration, whereas the internal intercostals muscles depress the rib cage to assist with expiration.

stem: What is a large, dome-shaped muscle that separates the thoracic cavity from the abdominal cavity?
rationale: The diaphragm is a large, dome-shaped muscle that separates the thoracic cavity from the abdominal cavity. When the diaphragm relaxes, it allows a decrease in thoracic volume.

stem: What statement about the heart is wrong?
choices: Contraction of the chambers is referred to as diastole, and relaxation as systole.
The heart muscle consists of four chambers.
Deoxygenated blood returns from all parts of the body through the venous system.
The right ventricle pumps the blood through the pulmonary valve to the pulmonary artery and into the lungs.
Blood is ejected past the aortic valve into the aorta, which supplies the entire body through the arterial system.
rationale: Contraction of the chambers is referred to as systole, and relaxation as diastole.

stem: Please check all the organs and/or muscles that lie completely within the thoracic ribs.
Which is the deepest of the abdominal muscles?
rationale: The transversus abdominis is the deepest of the abdominal muscles. All the abdominal muscles work together in performing defecation, urination, and forced expiration.

stem: What statement is true of the kidneys?
choices: The right kidney sits lower than the left kidney to allow space for the heart.
The lowermost surfaces of the kidneys are connected to the diaphragm by strong, ligamentous fibers.
The right kidney is higher than the left because of the pressure of the diaphragm.
The kidneys lie anterior to the abdominal cavity.
The primary function of the kidneys is to filter metabolic wastes and drugs from the blood.
rationale: The primary function of the kidneys is to filter metabolic wastes, ions, and drugs from the blood and expel them from the body via urination. The uppermost surfaces of the kidneys are connected to the diaphragm by strong, ligamentous fibers.

stem: What organ secretes bile?
rationale: The liver secretes bile, which is essential in neutralizing and diluting stomach acid and for digesting fat in the small intestine during the digestive process. The liver is the largest internal organ of the body.

stem: What organ produces insulin and glucagon?
rationale: The pancreas produces insulin and glucagon, which are hormones that control the amount of glucose and amino acids in the blood. It is located between the small intestine and the spleen.

stem: What statement is true of the gallbladder?
choices: It produces antibodies and lymphocytes for immunological function.
It detoxifies harmful chemicals and processes nutrients.
It serves as a reservoir of red blood cells.
It serves as a storage reservoir for bile secreted from the liver.
rationale: The gallbladder serves as a storage reservoir for bile secreted from the liver. It is a pear-shaped, saclike structure located on the inferior surface of the liver.

stem: What statement is true of the spleen?
choices: It serves as a storage reservoir for bile.
It regulates the number of red blood cells circulating in the human body.
It is the smallest lymphatic organ in the body.
It absorbs and stores excess glucose and detoxifies harmful chemicals.
rationale: The spleen’s main functions are to regulate the number of red blood cells circulating in the human body, to serve as a reservoir of red blood cells, to destroy ineffective red cells, to produce antibodies for immunological function, and to produce lymphocytes. It is the largest lymphatic organ in the body.

stem: Why are traumatic injuries to female reproductive organs rare?
choices: The female reproductive organs lie deeper in the abdominal viscera than male reproductive organs.
The female reproductive organs are located internally, as opposed to the male genitalia.
Female athletics usually do not involve full-contact activities.
The female reproductive organs are protected by the pelvis.
Female athletics usually do not involve high-collision activities.
rationale: Traumatic injuries to the female reproductive organs are rare because they are well protected by the pelvis.

stem: What step is the most appropriate for minimizing injuries to the thorax and abdominal region?
choices: athletes should eat meals an hour before activity.
athletes should urinate immediately before stepping onto the field.
athletes should wear shoulder pads that lie just above the sternum.
athletes should wear rib protectors that cover half of the thoracic cage.
athletes should consume large amounts of fluids before activity.
rationale: Meals should be eaten at least three to four hours before activity to allow foods to clear the stomach, and emptying the bladder before activity.

stem: In the history section of a thorax and abdomen assessment after a patient was injured, what would be considered an INAPPROPRIATE question to ask the patient?
choices: What position were you in when hit?
Have you had any muscle spasms?
How long has it been since you have eaten?
Was the bladder full or empty?
rationale: Asking if the patient uses laxatives may be more appropriate if the patient did not know the mechanism of injury and was complaining of a general medical condition.

stem: In the history section of a thorax and abdomen assessment after a patient was injured, what would be considered an inappropriate action to perform?
choices: observing if the patient is holding the chest wall
noting the position of the patient
rationale: While watching the patient’s facial expressions and rigidity of the abdominal wall is part of the assessment, it is more appropriate to perform during the palpation, or special tests section, of the assessment.

stem: What would be a wrong body structure to attempt to palpate during the palpation section of the thorax and abdomen assessment?
rationale: The ureters, small tubes that extend inferiorly from the kidney to the urinary bladder and store urine, are not palpable.

stem: What sign may also accompany intraabdominal bleeding?
rationale: The athletic trainer can produce rebound tenderness by pressing firmly on the abdomen and then quickly releasing pressure, which causes intense pain.

stem: What injury sustained in the region of the thorax and abdomen has an etiology of a direct blow to the anterolateral aspect of the thorax or indirect mechanism from a sudden twist or a fall on some object that compresses the rib cage?
rationale: A costochondral separation or dislocation has a higher incidence than do fractures.

stem: What injury sustained in the region of the thorax and abdomen could be managed with rest and support and treated symptomatically with an X-ray to rule out any complications?
rationale: If a rib fracture is suspected, an X-ray should be taken. Management for the injury itself is treated according to the symptoms presented, along with support, rest, and a possible rib brace on an as-needed basis.

stem: What injury commonly occurs in automobile accidents and results from a high-impact injury to the chest?
rationale: A fracture of the sternum results from a high-impact blow to the chest. They are most likely to occur in automobile accidents than in athletics.

stem: Violent up and down and lateral movements of the breasts can result in stretching to which of the following structures?
rationale: Cooper’s ligament can be damaged by uncontrolled movement of the breast over a period of time, which can lead to premature ptosis of the breasts.

stem: What is the most common type of cancer in women?
rationale: Breast cancer is the most common type of cancer in women and is the second leading cause of death by cancer in women.

stem: When a patient is hyperventilating, what should immediately be done?
choices: attempt to decrease the rate of carbon dioxide loss
attempt to decrease the rate of oxygen loss
attempt to increase the rate of carbon dioxide loss
attempt to increase the rate of respiration
have the patient breath in and out through their mouth
rationale: The immediate treatment of hyperventilation is to decrease the rate of carbon dioxide loss.

stem: What is the most common cause of sudden death syndrome in athletes under the age of 35?
rationale: In individuals 35 years and younger, the most common cause of exercise-induced sudden death is some congenital cardiovascular abnormality.

stem: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is a condition that is categorized by what?
choices: a disorder of the mitral valve
rationale: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy is a condition in which there is thickened cardiac muscle.

stem: How does commotio cordis occur?
rationale: Commotio cordis is cardiac arrest that occurs due to blunt impact to the chest.

stem: What statement is true of athletic heart syndrome?
choices: It results in a decrease of maximal stroke volume and cardiac output.
It is characterized by a decrease in resting heart rate.
It is most likely to occur in athletes who train for lesser than one hour a day.
It most often requires corrective surgery as a treatment.
It occurs more often in women than in men.
rationale: Individuals who have athletic heart syndrome have bradycardia or a decreased heart rate and longer diastolic filling time. Athletic heart syndrome is essentially asymptomatic and requires no treatment.

stem: The figure demonstrates what lung injury?
rationale: A hemothorax is the presence of blood within the pleural cavity.

stem: Why are adolescents at great risk for a commotio cordis injury?
choices: Their body structure is more pliable.
Their body structure is more vascular.

stem: What structure is wrong as a layer of the epidermis?
rationale: The epidermis is the outermost layer of the skin and itself is composed of several layers, which does not include the stratum cellulosum.

stem: What is the purpose of the stratum corneum layer within the epidermis?
choices: It acts as a permeability barrier allowing only small molecules to diffuse into the lower regions of the epidermis.
It is a barrier that protects against shearing forces.
It is a permeable layer that allows moisture and heat to escape.
It houses the pigment melanin which blocks harmful solar radiation.
It is part of the lymphatic system by collecting and presenting bacterial proteins.
rationale: This action of the stratum corneum protects against environmental irritants, toxins, and pathogens.

stem: What causes "goosebumps" or “standing-on-end” effect of bodily hair?
choices: The eccrine glands contract by stimuli such as cold or fear.
The apocrine glands contract by stimuli such as cold or fear.
The sebaceous glands contract by stimuli such as cold or fear.
The arrectores pilorum contract by stimuli such as cold or fear.
The dermal papilla contract by stimuli such as cold or fear.
rationale: This involuntary contraction pulls the hair into a vertical position, thus increasing the insulating effect of hair and protects the body from cold.

stem: What is contained within the subcutis layer of the skin?
rationale: Subcutaneous fat has a role in temperature regulation/insulation and energy storage, and it increases the mobility of the skin over the underlying tissues.

stem: The skin lesion called café´ au lait spots may be indicative of what disorder?
rationale: Where the café au lait spots are not harmful, the presence of the spots may be a sign of the genetic disorder of the nervous system that primarily affects the development and growth of nerve cell tissues.

stem: What skin trauma condition has an etiology of improperly fitting shoes and may have the presence of hammertoes?
rationale: Clavus durus, or hard corns, is the most serious type of corn and can share the same mechanism of injury as calluses.

stem: What skin trauma condition has signs and symptoms relative to a separation of the keratin from the granular layer of the epidermis, causing an oozing wound that develops into lesions that crust and crack?
rationale: Chafing of the skin can stem from friction or from rubbing the skin unduly and cause maceration of the skin in a climate of heat and moisture.

stem: What skin trauma conditions would be managed with bathing in tepid water and showering once a day; using moisturizing soaps and emollients; referral to physician for topical corticosteroids?
rationale: Xerotic, or dry skin, will appear dry with variable redness and scaling, occurring first on the shins, forearms, backs of the hands, and face with possible itching, cracking, and fissures.

stem: When managing hyperkeratosis of the feet and/or hands, what should be avoided?
rationale: Using pumice stone or emery files should be discouraged since their use results in frictional forces that stimulate the skin to produce additional callus.

stem: What is a common wound cleansing technique involving direct contact of a gauze with tissues to remove debris and bacteria?
rationale: Scrubbing and swabbing involves direct contact of a soft brush or gauze with tissues to remove debris and bacteria. The brush or gauze is moistened with a cleansing solution and wiped across the tissues.

stem: In the context of common wound cleansing techniques, identify a true statement about whirlpool baths and soaks.
choices: It increases the risk of cross-contamination and maceration of tissues.
It is ineffective in lowering bacterial counts.
It has been shown to be effective in the removal of foreign bodies.
It is the preferred cleansing technique for open traumatic wounds.
It refers to the controlled flow of a solution across the wound bed and periwound tissues.
rationale: Whirlpool baths and soaks are the immersion of the body part and wound into a tub or container of a cleansing solution to hydrate and loosen contaminants and necrotic tissue from the wound bed. Whirlpool baths and soaks increase the risk of cross-contamination and maceration of the tissues.

stem: What should an athletic trainer do when providing initial care to an athlete suffering from a laceration that involves tissue approximation of superficial- to partial-thickness wound?
choices: Close the wound with dermal adhesives or wound closure strips.
Dress the wound with a non-occlusive dressing or occlusive dressing based on the amount of exudate.
Cover the wound with alcohol-soaked gauze and immediately refer to a physician for further cleansing.
Cleanse the wound with mild soap and apply hydrogen peroxide solution over the area.
Apply antibiotic ointment and place occlusive dressing over the antibiotic.
rationale: With tissue approximation of superficial- to partial-thickness wound, an athletic trainer should dress the wound with a non-occlusive dressing or occlusive dressing based on amount of exudate. Tissue approximation and closure will depend on wound length, width, depth, and location.

stem: What is the most appropriate cleansing agent for all traumatic wounds?
rationale: Normal saline is the most appropriate cleansing solution for all traumatic wounds. Tap water can also be used for traumatic wounds, except those that have bone or tendon exposed.

stem: What debridement techniques refer to the softening and digesting of necrotic tissue by the body’s mechanisms in a moist wound environment created under occlusive dressings?
rationale: Autolytic debridement is the softening and digesting of necrotic tissue by the body’s mechanisms in a moist wound environment created under occlusive dressings.This technique is painless and occurs over several days, but should not be used with infected wounds.

stem: In the context of primary wound dressings, what is a type of occlusive dressing indicated for wounds that produce minimal levels of exudate such as superficial-thickness abrasions?
rationale: As primary dressings, films and hydrogels are indicated for wounds that produce minimal levels of exudate such as superficial-thickness abrasions. Films trap moisture underneath the dressing and are non-absorbent.

stem: What statement is true about occlusive dressings?
choices: They are semipermeable and create a moist wound environment.
They include impregnated sterile gauze, adhesive strips, and wound closure strips.
They are less effective in healing wounds in comparison with non-occlusive dressings.
They lack barrier properties to microorganisms.
They are associated with increased levels of pain, when compared with non-occlusive dressings.
rationale: Occlusive dressings are semipermeable or impermeable, create a moist wound environment, and provide a barrier to microorganisms. Non-occlusive dressings possess high permeability, allow desiccation of the wound bed, and lack barrier properties to microorganisms.

stem: In the context of primary wound dressings, what non-occlusive dressing can be used with infected wounds and may be more cost-effective than other dressings based on the frequent dressing changes that are required?
rationale: As primary dressings, woven and nonwoven gauze can be used with infected wounds and may be more cost-effective than other dressings based on the frequent dressing changes that are required. Woven, nonwoven, and impregnated gauze are indicated for puncture wounds with cavities to eliminate dead space and promote healing from the base upward.

stem: In the context of occlusive dressings, identify a true statement about hydrogels.
choices: They are constructed with a high water content and can donate moisture to the wound.
They are ineffective in managing partial-thickness wounds with moderate exudate amounts.
They are designed for partial- to full-thickness wounds with heavy exudate.
They interact with exudate to produce a gel that conforms to the wound contours.
They trap moisture underneath the dressing and are non-absorbent.
rationale: Hydrogels are constructed with a high water content and can donate moisture to the wound. Partial-thickness wounds with moderate exudate amounts can be managed with hydrogels and hydrocolloids.

stem: What is a reasonable amount of time that can lapse for the application of sutures to put in a wound?
rationale: Sutures no longer have a “golden period” for wound closure, but a reasonable time period could be 18 hours following injury dependent on the type of wound.

stem: What would be a wrong statement for the use of an occlusive dressing in the management of a wound?
choices: Perceived pain from exposed nerve endings is lowered.
It is cost and time effective.
rationale: Scab formation is minimized with the use of any type of occlusive dressing, such as semipermeable films, hydrogels, and hydrocolloids.

stem: What bacterial infection has an etiology of being caused by a group A beta-hemolytic streptococci, S. aureus, or a combination of the two?
rationale: Impetigo contagiosa is spread rapidly when infected patients are in close contact with one another, such as during the activity of wrestling.

stem: What bacterial infection has signs and symptoms of a broad range dependent on the part of the body that is infected, but results in redness, swelling, and tenderness at the site of infection?
rationale: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus, or MRSA, may also not present with any symptoms.

stem: What skin trauma condition would be managed with the intermittent application of moist heat, antibiotic medication applied locally, as well as systematically, depending on the scope of the condition?
rationale: Management for folliculitis is similar to the treatment for impetigo. The moist heat helps increase circulation.

stem: Hidradenitis suppurativa can be described as a condition that affects what?
rationale: Hidradenitis suppurativa is a chronic inflammatory condition of the apocrine glands or large sweat glands commonly found in the axilla, scrotum, labia majora, and nipples.

stem: A patient discloses having taken the medications Retin-A and clindamycin. What is the patient’s skin condition?
rationale: Retin-A and clindamycin are useful in treating moderate acne or acne vulgaris.

stem: A soccer player splits open the forehead and needs five sutures. A few days later, the athlete complains of stiffness in the neck and is running a low-grade fever. What could be wrong with the patient?
rationale: The first sign of tetanus infection is stiffness of the jaw and muscles of the neck and fever can become markedly elevated.

stem: A patient presents with the skin condition demonstrated in the figure. It seems to be following an area that is consistent with dermatome T6. What could be wrong with this patient?
rationale: Herpes Zoster, or shingles, appears in a specific pattern on the body in an area that is innervated by a specific nerve root.

stem: If a sunscreen has an SPF of 20, what statement is correct?
choices: The person should apply the sunscreen every 20 minutes.
The person will receive 20% less sun than a person who is not wearing sunscreen.
The person will be 20% less likely to receive a sunburn.
It would require 20 times longer to sustain the same amount of UV radiation with the sunscreen than without.
It would require that a person applies the product 20 times in a day to avoid full UV radiation.
rationale: A SPF of 20 means that it would require 20 times longer to sustain the same amount of UV radiation with the sunscreen than without.

stem: A freshman volleyball player presents with a skin condition demonstrated in the figure. What is present?
rationale: A contact dermatitis is categorized by skin that reacts with redness, swelling, and the formation of vesicles that ooze fluid and form a crust.

stem: What insects can cause Lyme disease?
rationale: Ticks can cause Lyme disease, which is a serious illness with significant morbidity and mortality.

stem: A patient presents with pain in the abdomen and back. The patient also reports of a headache, anxiety, increased blood pressure, and difficulty breathing. The patient remembers being “bit by a bug,” but is unable to identify the insect. Based on the signs and symptoms, what could have bitten the patient?
rationale: A patient will present with the above-mentioned symptoms if they are bitten by a brown recluse spider.

stem: What characteristic of a mole would be considered wrong when trying to diagnose a melanoma?
rationale: Melanomas may show ABCDE characteristics that include asymmetry, border, color, diameter, and evolving.

stem: You notice that a patient is constantly scratching the head. Upon further investigation, you see the sores demonstrated in this figure. What could be wrong with this patient?
rationale: Pediculosis, or lice, is located near a hair shaft and causes an itching dermatitis, which provokes scratching and pustules.

stem: A patient has yellowish-white scales on the elbows, knees, and around the umbilicus. What skin condition is present?
rationale: Psoriasis is a common chronic disease of the skin that causes itching and the patient may have yellowish-white scales on their elbows, knees, and around their umbilicus. An example of this skin condition is demonstrated in this figure.

stem: Select only the skin disorders that could be contagious via skin or surface contact.
What condition presents with transient and peripheral vasoconstriction of digital arteries in response to cold temperatures?
rationale: Blood flow to the skin is restricted as a thermoregulatory mechanism to sustain the core body temperature and prevent loss of body heat. With Raynaud’s phenomenon, there can be primary and secondary, where both cause decreased blood flow to the skin or alter normal vessel reactivity to cold temperatures.

stem: What medications should a person with Ehlers-Danlos syndrome avoid taking?
rationale: Vascular Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is a more severe form of the disorder that can cause dissection or rupture of the blood vessels or hollow organs particularly of the intestines and uterus.

stem: What condition is present in the figure?
The immune response in which lymphocytes (T cells) are produced by the thymus in response to the antigen exposure is known as __________.
rationale: An immune that responds by disposing of the antigen and preventing damage may be cell- mediated.

stem: In the context of the immune system, what term refers to an invading agent?
rationale: Immunity means being protected from a disease by having been previously exposed to an invading agent, called an antigen.

stem: In the context of the management of an acute asthmatic attack, what would be a wrong action to perform?
choices: removing the patient from the environmental area
administering personalized medication prescribed by a physician
encouraging the patient to drink water
letting the patient perform controlled breathing along with relaxation exercises
massaging the patient’s hands and feet while the patient is in a supine position
rationale: Massaging the patient's hands and feet while in a supine position is not an appropriate action to perform while managing an acute asthmatic attack. Removing the patient from the environment that has triggered the attack, administering personalized medication prescribed by a physician, encouraging the patient to drink water, and letting the patient perform controlled breathing along with relaxation exercises are all actions that one is required to take while managing an acute asthmatic attack.

stem: Exercise-induced asthma is also referred to as __________.
rationale: Exercise-induced asthma (EIA) is also referred to as exercise-induced bronchospasm (EIB). It can be stimulated by exercise in some individuals, whereas in others, it may be provoked during moderate exercise.

stem: What is a hereditary disease where there is degeneration of skeletal muscle with an associated loss in strength and muscle tissue is gradually replaced by adipose and fibrous connective tissue?
rationale: Onset for Duchenne muscular dystrophy has an onset during early childhood, between the ages of two and ten years that may appear as the child learns to walk.

stem: What disorder is an autoimmune disease inflammatory of the central nervous system that causes permanent damage to the myelin sheath?
rationale: Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune inflammatory disease of the central nervous system that causes deterioration and permanent damage to the myelin sheath that surrounds a nerve cell axon.

stem: What is a nervous system disorder characterized by a high fever, stiff neck, intense headache, and sensitivity to light and sound, progressing to vomiting, convulsions, and coma?
rationale: Meningitis is an inflammation of the meninges, or membranes, that surround the spinal cord and brain that is caused by infection.

stem: If some individuals who have the sickle-cell trait may never be symptomatic, how does exposure to high altitudes warrant health risks to that individual?
choices: The cell shape, an oval, limits the ability to transport oxygen.
High altitude triggers a decrease in ambient oxygen.
Life span of a sickle cell is extended and fatigues easier.
The hypertrophic size of the cell allows too much passage through the blood vessels.
The unique cell shape inhibits clogging.
rationale: This characteristic crisis can also cause the patient to experience a headache and convulsions.

stem: Footstrike hemolysis is also known as __________.
rationale: Footstrike hemolysis is also known as runner's anemia. It is the second most prevalent cause of iron deficiency in athletes and is due to the impact of the foot as it strikes the surface. This impact force destroys normal erythrocytes within the vascular system.

stem: If diabetes is not treated adequately, or too little insulin is produced, a loss of sodium, potassium, and ketone bodies through excessive urination is likely to lead to __________.
rationale: If diabetes is not treated adequately, or too little insulin is produced, a loss of sodium, potassium, and ketone bodies through excessive urination is likely to lead to ketoacidosis. This can be life- threatening and can lead to coma.

stem: A patient who presents with dry mouth, increased thirst, frequent urination, hunger after eating, fatigue, and unexplained weight loss could be suffering from what?
rationale: A patient suffering from hyperthyroidism may present with increased metabolism, rapid fatigue during exercise, weight loss, and hyperthermia during exercise.

stem: How long would a petit mal seizure episode last?
rationale: Petit mal seizures are extremely brief and can last 5-15 seconds.

stem: What is the most common type of cancer in males?
rationale: Prostate cancer is the highest rate of incidence in males.

stem: What symptom is wrong as a warning sign of cancer?
choices: a change in bowel and bladder habits
an obvious change in a wart
rationale: An increase in appetite is not a warning sign of cancer.

stem: What sexually transmitted disease results in pelvic inflammatory disease and is an important cause of infertility and ectopic pregnancy?
rationale: Chlamydia trachomatis results in pelvic inflammatory disease and is an important cause of infertility and ectopic pregnancy.

stem: What STD has no cure?
rationale: Genital herpes currently has no cure, but symptoms can be treated with acyclovir.

stem: What STD will insidiously be present even though the outward signs are no longer present?
rationale: Despite being treated, gonorrhea will be present even though the outward signs are no longer present.

stem: Penicillin is the most effective current drug used to treat all stages of what STD?
rationale: Penicillin is currently the appropriate antibiotic for all stages of syphilis.

stem: Primary amenorrhea is defined as __________.
choices: the absence of a menstrual cycle
clots present during the menstrual cycle
rationale: Amenorrhea is the absence or suppression of menstruation.

stem: Endometriosis is defined as __________.
choices: the condition in which uterine cells grow outside the uterus
the rupture of the fallopian tube
rationale: Endometriosis is uterine cells growing outside of the uterus.

stem: If a female patient is having pain during sexual intercourse, abnormal bleeding, and painful menstrual cycles, what could the patient be experiencing?
rationale: The above accurately describes the signs and symptoms of an ovarian cyst.

stem: What is the recommended daily allowance of calcium for adolescent females?
rationale: Calcium nutrition is needed for female bone health and the recommended daily allowance for adolescent females is 1,200 mg/day.

stem: What is a contraindication to exercise during pregnancy?
rationale: Cervix insufficiency or cerclage (a procedure in which the cervical opening is closed with stitches to prevent or delay preterm birth) is a contraindication for performing exercise during pregnancy.

stem: In the context of contraception and reproduction, an intrauterine device made of copper lasts for __________.
rationale: An intrauterine device made of copper lasts for 10 to 12 years. However, another type that releases the hormone progestin lasts for 3 years.

stem: Use of oral contraceptive pills related to physical performance should be __________.
choices: under the express direction of a physician
accompanied by the insertion of an intrauterine device
accompanied by the insertion of the NuvaRing
accompanied by a transdermal birth-control patch
rationale: Use of oral contraceptive pills related to physical performance should be under the express direction of a physician. Under physician care, they have been prescribed to delay menstruation during competition. However, oral contraceptive use is acceptable for females with no medical problems who have coitus at least twice a week.

stem: If a patient presents with fruity smelling breath, which condition is present?
rationale: Fruity smelling breath, nausea, vomiting, thirst, flushed skin, and mental confusion are all signs and symptoms of a diabetic ketoacidosis.

stem: Please check all of the symptoms that are most commonly associated with the flu.
What condition is present in the figure?
rationale: The rash first appears as individual red crops or spots, each of which progressively evolves through stages of macules, papules, vesicles, and crusts over a period of 2 to 3 days. The rash begins on the back and chest.

stem: Acute, seasonal allergic condition that results from airborne pollens, dust, dander, or mold is referred to as __________.
rationale: Pollinosis, or hay fever, is an acute seasonal allergic condition that results from airborne pollens, dust, dander, or mold.

stem: Please check all of the signs and symptoms of asthma.
choices: expansion of the rib spaces as the patient inhales

stem: What is a wrong warning sign of cancer?
choices: a change in bowel or bladder habits
rationale: Specific signs of cancer can vary tremendously, depending on the type of cancer, and warrant immediate attention by a physician.

stem: What is the danger of sickle cell anemia?
choices: the sickle cell shape inhibits the ability for oxygen to be stored.
sickling of the cell can result in malaria.
a sickled cell has a shorter life span causing severe anemia.
sickle cells create the sickle cell trait.
it can result in decreased iron.
rationale: The short life of the sickle cell can produce severe anemia in individuals with acute sickle cell anemia. The cell’s distorted shape inhibits its passage through the small blood vessels and can cause clustering of the cells and, consequently, clogging of the blood vessels and thus ischemia.

stem: Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) was formally known as __________.
rationale: CRPS was previously called RSD or causalgia. It is an abnormal and excessive response of the sympathetic portion of the autonomic nervous system that occurs following injury.

stem: Management of what condition may require hospitalization, supplemental oxygen, and medication using a combination of antivirals and anti-inflammatories?
rationale: Most patients will recover within 2 weeks; however, in severe cases, both infants and adults may require these treatment methods. Powered by TCPDF (www.tcpdf.org)

stem: In the history section of a foot assessment, what would be considered an INAPPROPRIATE question to ask the patient?
choices: Is this the first time this condition has occurred?
Was the mechanism of injury a sudden strain, twist, or blow to the foot?
Do you have a dorsalis pedal pulse?
Can you point to an exact site of pain?
rationale: Asking the patient if he/she has dorsalis pedal pulse would be better achieved during the “palpation” of the assessment.
